column1: # A claim id is <doc-file-stem>#<slug>; each entry must be referenced by a

column2: A pinned DocState is unaffected by later transactions

A commit touching one object path-copies only the nodes on its path; every untouched record is shared by SlotRef identity between the pre- and post-commit versions, so live-slot growth is O(path depth), not O(document)
Dropping the last pin to a superseded version and draining reclaims exactly that version's unique nodes and no node still shared with the live version
A transaction with multiple mutations produces exactly one published version (revision +1) and calls the commit sink exactly once; current() is unchanged until commit
A dropped or aborted transaction leaves current()/revision() unchanged, emits no journal entry or damage, and reclaims its working records after drain()
commit() flushes the union (deduped by object) of per-mutation damage to the DamageSink in exactly one call; abort() flushes none
A non-coalesced commit notifies the CommitSink exactly once with the assembled entry: name, per-object (before, after) record edges, per-content (before, after) state-handle pairs, and the damage set

N consecutive commits sharing a non-zero coalescing key merge into exactly one journal entry (first-before / last-after, unioned damage and object set) while each commit still publishes a new revision

An undo() publishes a new version (revision +1) that rebinds every touched object to the entry's before edge and flushes the entry's damage through the DamageSink exactly once, without notifying the CommitSink and with no special output path; redo() is the symmetric after re-application

commit -> undo -> redo returns the published version's object-record edges to the same SlotRef identities as the post-commit version and returns the cursor to the tip; a full undo-to-base then redo-to-tip round-trips live_slots() with no leak

Undo/redo only move the cursor and never rewrite a stored entry; a fresh non-coalescing commit while the cursor is not at the tip discards the redo tail and appends but leaves every surviving entry byte-identical

When the accumulated cost (record sizes + registered StateCostFn) exceeds the budget, the journal drops oldest entries from the tail until within budget (never below one entry); after a drain() the uniquely-superseded records those entries pinned are reclaimed (live_slots() falls) and can_undo() reflects the reduced depth

A run of commits sharing a non-zero coalescing key collapses into a single tip entry, so one undo() reverts the whole gesture to its pre-gesture state, while each commit had published its own revision

A pinned DocState keeps every content object's captured StateHandle live and resolvable: a later set_content_state + publish on the same object leaves the pinned version's content_state(id) unchanged and does not release the pinned handle; only dropping the last version and journal reference (then drain()) releases it

Publishing a ContentRecord embedding a non-none StateHandle retains it through the StateRefSink exactly once; reclaiming that record slot (zero-count, after drain()) releases it exactly once; an untouched content object shared across versions incurs no additional retain

A coalesced gesture (N set_content_state commits under one non-zero coalescing key) leaves only the first-before and last-after handles referenced at the tip; the intermediate captured handles are released after drain(), and one undo() fires the RestoreSink once per content object with the pre-gesture before handle

Attaching a layer at an index, detaching a layer, and reordering a layer produce exactly the intended bottom-to-top ObjectId sequence read back through the composition-scoped accessor; reorder is a stable move that preserves the relative order of untouched members

A composition with more than k_max_inline_layers members stores its order in HAMT-backed chunk objects and reports the correct full order; migrating up across the cap and back down is lossless (round-trip equality), and a spilled membership edit path-copies only the touched chunk map-paths (untouched chunks shared by SlotRef identity across versions)

Each attach/detach/reorder raises the revision by exactly one and contributes exactly one damage record for the composition id, spanning the whole composition (infinite region) at all instants (all() range), flushed once at commit
Undo of an attach/detach/reorder restores the prior layer order exactly (including across the spill boundary) and redo re-applies it, as ordinary journal navigation publishes with no new entry type

A committed content removal (Transaction::remove of a content id, coalesced with the layer's detach_layer in one commit) is undone by Model::navigate: undo restores the erased ContentRecord to the same SlotRef identity its owning edge had before the removal (structural identity, not just equal bytes) and re-links every untouched sibling by identity; redo re-erases it; a version pinned before the removal resolves the content unchanged across the whole cycle; and a full undo->redo round-trips live_slots() after drain() with no leak

Document::remove_content deletes an editable content and its referencing layer(s) in one transaction (revision +1, one damage flush) and keeps EditableBinding::unrouted_state_calls() == 0 across a remove->undo->redo->commit-new-edit sequence because the content's binding row is retained (bound_count() unchanged, bound(id) still true) while the journal holds the removal's before edge; an undo re-resolves the restored content with its captured state intact (a set_content_state reachable through the restored record)

Document::add_content commits exactly one new published version (revision +1) minting a ContentRecord that embeds the caller-supplied kind and, for content with no editable facet, an inert (k_state_none) StateHandle (an editable content's record embeds its captured initial state instead, in that same single version -- see #editable-runtime-bound), reachable in the published DocState via find_content(id) under the returned id; a content with no referencing layer stays a live top-level map entry

After the migration the id->Content binding is served by the runtime side-map (resolve(id) returns the exact live Content* that was added) while the versioned record exposes only the opaque {kind, state} carrying no vtable pointer, and a DocState pinned before the add never observes the new record

Runtime-synthesized identities (an operator's inline input children) are drawn from a reserved half of the 64-bit ObjectId space that the model allocator never issues, so a synthesized cache identity never equals any model ObjectId in the document -- damage or cache invalidation naming a model id cannot evict a synthesized id's tiles, and no synthesized id is ever journaled or serialized
A placement edit (transform, opacity, order) auto-emits exactly one damage for the edited object, spanning the whole object (infinite region) at all instants (all() range), flushed once at commit

A HostViewport::step samples composition_time from its playhead policy, drains accumulated model damage into render_frame, and honors FrameOutcome::schedule_follow_up: a free-running viewport advances its transport by exactly round_ties_even(real_elapsed*rate) (delegating to Transport::advance), while an audio-mastered viewport advances the transport zero times and derives composition_time from the device monitor's playhead_snapshot().

A HostViewport constructed against a Document derives its content resolution, its damage-sink install, and its external-arrival settle hook from that document, with the host supplying none of them and never holding a reference to the versioned model: the viewport resolves the document's contents and composites them byte-identically to a hand-wired Model-and-resolver viewport; a commit's damage reaches its frame with no set_damage_sink call by the host; a deferred external arrival settles within the very step that observes it, dropping pending_external_loads() to zero, publishing a new revision, and issuing the frame that composites it; and a router-configured viewport registers with the DamageRouter instead of occupying the document's single sink slot.

A single DamageRouter occupies the model's one damage-sink slot and fans each flushed damage batch out to N registered HostViewports, once per viewport in registration order; every viewport drains that batch into its own frame; a zero-registrant router flushes a no-op; destroying one viewport's RAII registration stops delivery to it while the others keep receiving subsequent commits.

A caller-supplied content damage's finite region and time-range survive mutation, commit, flush, journal entry, and undo/redo replay bit-identically; the model adds no coarse floor that widens or clobbers them
Structural (placement/graph) damage carries an unbounded all() time-range, so a temporal consumer such as the audio lookahead window never skips a structural edit
Compositing is source-over on premultiplied alpha in the working space

Every surface the compositor allocates in a frame -- the frame target and each per-layer temp -- carries the composition's configured working-space tag triple; the working space is per-composition model configuration read from the pinned version, not a driver hardcode, and an unstorable configured working space surfaces as an error rather than a crash
Every surface exposes its complete tag triple (pixel format, color space, premultiplication) from creation, and the reference backend refuses to composite across mismatched tags

Compositing an 8-bit sRGB surface decodes to linear working light, blends there on premultiplied alpha, and re-encodes, so the result is the linear-light source-over, decisively not the gamma-space blend of the stored bytes
The 8-bit sRGB fast-mode codec round-trips every one of the 256 codes per color channel (sRGB transfer) and every 8-bit alpha (linear unorm) exactly: encode(decode(x)) == x

Software half-float conversion is portable (no _Float16 reliance) and correct on the IEEE edge cases -- signed zero, subnormals, infinities, NaN payloads, 1.0, 0.5 -- and every non-NaN representable half round-trips half->float->half exactly while float->half stays within one f16 ULP

A CPU surface's byte storage is width*height*bytes_per_pixel(format) -- 16/8/4 bytes per pixel for rgba32f/rgba16f/rgba8 -- and a checked typed span reinterprets it only when the requested format matches the tag, else yields an empty span
Format conversion routes format->premultiplied linear working float->format (2N codecs, never N*N pairs): an rgba8<->rgba32f round-trip through the working space is byte-exact for opaque samples

Filtered resampling -- the Catmull-Rom composite tap and the Lanczos-3 exact-2:1 rung downsample (superseding the walking skeleton's bilinear tap and box 2:1 mean) -- interpolates decoded premultiplied linear working floats (never encoded bytes, never straight alpha), so an antialiased Rgba8Srgb edge resampled shows the linear-light result, decisively not the gamma-space / straight-alpha resample of the stored bytes

A nested child whose composition declares a different working space composites its own layers in its own declared working space and the composed result converts exactly once into the parent's working space at the boundary -- decisively not blending the child's layers in the parent's space, and decisively not one conversion per child layer

A nested child whose working space equals the parent's issues zero conversions and allocates no boundary intermediate: the homogeneous render is byte-identical to, and allocates exactly as much as, the render that predates the conversion path
A backend that cannot store a requested SurfaceFormat returns a SurfaceError value, never null and never aborting; a supported format returns a live surface carrying that exact tag triple

Backend::convert rewrites the source surface into the destination's tag triple position-for-position and replaces every destination pixel: it takes no transform and no opacity, blends nothing, and for equal tags is an exact byte-for-byte copy -- decisively not a source-over composite and decisively not a decode/encode round-trip

A backend's advertised BackendCaps match its behavior: the reference backend advertises cpu_access and its stored surfaces yield a non-empty CPU span, while import_handles is empty and sync_primitives false until surfaces.import / GPU backends land the machinery
A released pooled surface is reused for a subsequent same-(size, format) acquisition rather than reallocated; the backend allocates once per distinct (size, format), not once per request

When a RenderResult carries a provided surface, the compositor composites/caches from it instead of the request's target, honoring the request's region and scale; the untouched target is returned to the pool

A provided surface is refcounted; the compositor releases its reference (invoking the content release callback when it drops the last one) once it has composited or copied the surface, and never before
A transient provided surface is copied into a cache-owned surface when cached and never retained across the frame boundary; the cached tile is independent of the content surface

A forwarding Backend test double delegates every Backend operation to its inner backend and adds no pixel behavior of its own -- an operation the double does not explicitly override is forwarded, decisively not silently no-oped

Backend::clear_rect and Backend::composite_clipped write no pixel outside their device-space clip rect: the clip is intersected with the destination bounds (a clip reaching past the edge is legal, not an error), an empty clip is a no-op, and a clip covering the whole destination is byte-identical to the unclipped clear/composite -- which is how the unclipped operations are defined, so a backend carries one kernel per operation rather than two
CPU backend rendering is byte-exact deterministic

Each format-templated color kernel (fill, source-over/bilinear, box downsample, cross-format convert) and its codecs produce byte-identical output for fixed inputs across the supported compilers, per format, with no tolerance

Minification through a mip pyramid uses a fixed 6-tap Lanczos-3 half-band decimation and magnification uses an interpolating Catmull-Rom bicubic tap, both over decoded premultiplied linear working floats -- decisively not the 2x2 box average and 4-tap bilinear they replace: a high-frequency input decimates to different bytes than a box filter produces, and a fractional-phase magnification differs from a bilinear tap, while at integer phase the interpolating cubic reproduces the source sample bit-exactly. Weights are tabulated float32 with a fixed tap order and no runtime libm, so every rung and every fractional phase is a byte-exact deterministic function of the decoded buffer; negative-lobe undershoot is clamped to non-negative per channel

A scale-ladder rung produced by CpuBackend::downsample is byte-identical, at every interior pixel whose 6-tap support stays in range, to the matching mip level kind_raster builds from the same source pixels -- both run the one arbc::media Lanczos-3 half-band bank (doc 07's "shared by the kinds' mip pyramids and the backend's compositing kernels" made testable), and the shared-bank parity holds where the two consumers' edge conventions (compositor zero-border vs kind clamp-to-edge) do not diverge
A released slot is the next same-class allocation's perfect hole; fixed slabs make fragmentation impossible
Slot pointers taken before growth stay valid and unchanged across arbitrary chunk growth
Releasing a file-backed chunk hole-punches it, dropping the workspace file's allocated block count
Pin/unpin traffic touches only the parallel count tables, never a data page, so data chunks mprotected read-only never fault under arbitrary reference churn
Releasing the last count with a deferred sink installed only enqueues the slot: the destructor has not run and the slot is still live; only drain() runs ~T and returns the slot to the free list

Dropping the root of an N-deep retained-SlotRef chain and draining once runs all N destructors exactly once and returns the arena to its pre-build live count, with the C++ cascade stack bounded independent of N (iterative, not recursive)
An ordered commit (msync data, flip the A/B root, msync the header) leaves recovery on the old root before the header sync and the new root after it, each resolving a consistent graph
A slot (or emptied chunk) freed after the last checkpoint is not re-allocated (nor hole-punched) until a commit makes the freeing durable, then becomes reusable
In a debug build, a write through a pointer into a data chunk published by a checkpoint faults, because the checkpoint mprotects published chunks read-only

Every workspace-file syscall failure (grow / msync / hole-punch / header I/O) and every truncated or short file surfaces as a WorkspaceFileErrc value, never an abort or UB; the file remains recoverable to its last durable root

A const&/peek traversal of a pinned version follows in-record SlotRef edges to the data without touching a count, so refcount chunks mprotected read-only never fault during a full traversal (whereas a by-value shared_ptr visit, which dirties a count per node, would)

Several typed views over one size-class slab store share a single per-slot refcount column (and one debug generation column); a slot recycled from one type to another reuses the same count entry, and its generation bump is visible to a stale reference of the original type

A non-writer release pushes the slot onto a thread-local free pool (no arena growth, no global lock on the push) and it returns to the writer's allocator only via the global spill; a writer-allocates-while-a-second-thread-releases run recycles every slot with no loss and no duplication and the arena returns to baseline
A thread's just-released slot is the one its next same-thread allocation returns, from its thread-local pool before any global-pool round-trip occurs

Reopening a workspace file that backs several size-class stores re-binds each store to exactly the chunks the arena directory records as its own, in slot order, so every record resolves to its pre-crash bytes; routing is by the recorded owner tag, never by chunk byte size (two stores of different strides can share one chunk size)

The per-store high-water a recovery reads is the one published by the root it selected: a kill anywhere between the data msync and the header msync recovers the old root paired with the old store-table snapshot, never a mismatched pair

A workspace whose root-slot page landed durably but whose store-table snapshot page did not (an unordered partial writeback of the header) opens on the older root whose snapshot generation matches it, restoring that root's high-waters intact, rather than pairing the new root with a stale snapshot and silently under-restoring

Opening a workspace whose store table disagrees with the reopening build's slot stride / alignment / slots-per-chunk yields a WorkspaceFileErrc value and no mapping, rather than silently mis-routing chunks

A producer thread churning version pins (retain/release) on the shared interior nodes of a published version and a consumer thread peek-traversing that version run concurrently on one size-class store with the data chunks mprotected read-only: the producer's count-only writes never fault a read-only data page and the consumer observes no torn value, so the two threads' page-write and page-read sets are disjoint under real contention -- the concurrent composition of #const-ref-traversal-touches-no-refcount-page and #refcounts-outside-data-pages

After reopening a workspace file, every reachable slot's refcount equals its live in-degree, reconstructed by the model's typed reachability walk over the DocState HAMT with no count read from disk; witnessed by post-recovery reclamation freeing exactly the unique slots (no leak, no double-free) -- the same arithmetic an anonymous twin of the document produces

Reopening a document's workspace file after a crash at any syscall boundary in the checkpoint protocol yields the last DURABLE committed version, field-identical (records, composition membership order, spill chain) and with counts rebuilt so it keeps editing; a crash before the header msync lands on the prior root, never a half-published mixture of the two

A checkpoint of an unchanged scene (no slot allocated, none freed, no chunk grown since the last one) issues zero data msyncs but still flips and syncs the header, so the durable root advances at no data-flush cost

Content::render is a pure function of (snapshot state, region, scale, time): two calls with an identical RenderRequest (same snapshot, region, scale, time) yield byte-identical target pixels, and the snapshot handle is a real input -- two requests differing only in snapshot yield different pixels

Content::render answers along one code path: it either returns a RenderResult inline or returns nullopt and settles later via the supplied RenderCompletion. Driving a synchronous content (returns a value) and an asynchronous content (returns nullopt, later completes) through the identical render->settle->take() path yields equivalent, byte-identical settlements

A Content that overrides none of the operator-graph members is a graph leaf: inputs() returns an empty span, identity() returns nullopt for every request, and map_input_damage(i, r) returns r unchanged (the identity map)

A RenderCompletion delivers exactly one settlement: complete/fail are mutually exclusive and a second settle attempt is ignored; take() yields the settlement once. cancelled() is an advisory cooperative flag -- cancel() makes cancelled() observe true but does not prevent a subsequent settlement, and this holds under concurrent complete/cancel from different threads
Eviction removes the least-recently-used unpinned entry from the lowest-populated priority class first; a higher-class entry is never evicted while a lower-class evictable entry remains
A pinned entry is never evicted; the pin keeps it resident past the byte budget, and a removed-while-pinned entry value is held until its last hold releases
Inserting past the byte budget evicts (LRU within priority class) until resident bytes fit or only pinned entries remain; the budget bounds resident bytes except for the pinned working set

The tile cache key carries revision (edit axis) and achieved_time (playback axis) independently; Static content omits achieved_time so no still grows the cache, and two entries differing only in achieved_time (including present-vs-absent) or only in revision are distinct keys

The tile cache key is (content id, revision, scale rung, tile coords[, achieved_time]); the value is a backend surface plus {achieved_scale, exact} metadata whose byte cost is width * height * bytes_per_pixel(format)

The audio block cache is the tile cache with a 1D key (content id, revision, block index, rate); block keys differing in any single field are distinct, and both tile and block keys instantiate the same KeyedStore machinery

A fresh composition defaults its working audio format to k_working_audio (48 kHz, stereo); set_working_audio_format publishes a new revision whose value is visible on the pinned version and leaves earlier pinned versions unchanged; it is a no-op on an absent or non-composition id

org.arbc.tone renders a procedural tone at any requested sample rate: render_audio at rates {8000, 22050, 44100, 48000, 96000} reports achieved_rate == request.sample_rate and exact == true under both BestEffort and Exact, never degrading below a native rate -- the procedural side of doc 12's resolution/rate-independence symmetry

org.arbc.nested's audio facet aggregates its child composition's audio by pulling each audible child layer through PullService::pull_audio (never child->audio()->render_audio directly), additively mixing each contribution scaled by the layer's gain and time-mapped (varispeed) through the layer's time_map at the composed rational rate; a nested-of-tones scene produces samples byte-identical to mixing those tones directly at top level under the equivalent monitor -- the rendering-is-recursion identity for audio -- and each descent carries the outer request's snapshot and exactness verbatim, one pull per audible child layer

org.arbc.nested's audio_stability() is Static iff every reachable audible child-layer audio facet is Static (Live dominates, else Timed), audio_extent() is the time-mapped union of the reachable child extents (nullopt if any reachable child audio is Static-unbounded), and both recompute only when the child's aggregate revision changes (zero recomputes across repeated queries at a stable revision, one re-key on a bump), extending the visual aggregate-revision memo

At the nesting boundary org.arbc.nested reconstructs a child that reports achieved_rate < the composed request rate with a deterministic fixed-tap windowed-sinc/polyphase filter over decoded float32 samples -- a band-limited reconstruction, decisively not a nearest/hold -- producing exactly `frames` samples at the composed rate before the additive mix; the reconstruction is a byte-exact deterministic function of (native samples, native rate, composed rate, window), one pull for a rate-honoring child and exactly two (discovery + a block-cache-served native re-request) for a below-rate child, and it preserves honesty: the aggregate achieved_rate still reports the child's native rate and exact stays false

The composition mixer walks a composition's layers bottom-to-top and additively sums each audible in-span layer's audio -- pulled through PullService::pull_audio (never render_audio directly), scaled by gain, varispeed time-mapped through the layer's composed rational time_map at the composed rate, and remixed to the working layout -- into a silence-initialized target; a composition of tones produces samples byte-identical to summing those tone renders directly, and achieved_rate/exact fold as min/conjunction over contributors

PullService::pull_audio serves a resident exact-fresh block from the 1D block cache with zero dispatch, dispatches a miss exactly once carrying the request's snapshot/exactness/rate verbatim, honors the recursion-depth budget, and settles the AudioCompletion exactly once on every path -- the audio arm of pull

Layers whose content has no audio facet, or that are !audible()/gain<=0/out-of-span, contribute exactly zero and issue zero pull_audio dispatches; the mixer issues exactly one dispatch per audible in-span layer with an audio facet

The lookahead scheduler keeps a ring of prepared mixed blocks covering [playhead, playhead+horizon), each produced by mix_composition on a worker; drain hands out a prepared block when ready and silence plus an underrun count otherwise, and never invokes mix_composition/render_audio on the drain thread; a fully-primed ring drained in order is byte-identical to mix_composition called directly per output window, and identical between worker_count==0 and worker_count>0

A seek/set_rate/direction change flushes prepared blocks no longer ahead of the new playhead and re-enumerates the want-list; blocks whose output window is unchanged and un-damaged are retained, not re-mixed -- the re-mix count equals only the count of newly-needed blocks

Priming classifies each contributor's decode-behind blocks onto the temporal prefetch ring (prime_ring, PriorityClass::Temporal, in playback direction) and, via the bound PullConfig::blocks/audio_dispatch, populates the BlockCache, so a subsequent pull_audio for those BlockKeys hits with zero dispatch (closing the mix-engine no-fill gap)

A damage time-range intersecting the lookahead window invalidates exactly the prepared output blocks and cached BlockKeys it overlaps and forces their re-mix on the next prime; non-overlapping prepared blocks are retained

The lookahead fill warms the transitive contributor closure -- recursive descent through nested compositions and the below-rate native re-request a resampling boundary provokes -- dispatching a contributor only once its own closure is resident and mixing an output block only at full transitive residency; the threaded fill (worker_count > 0) is byte-identical to the inline fill for nested and below-rate scenes, never mixing silence for a not-yet-rendered descendant (extends 12-audio#lookahead-prepares-ahead-of-playhead's flat worker_count identity and 12-audio#lookahead-fills-block-cache-through-prefetch-ring's single-level prime)

A contributor declaring a constant processing latency() = k makes the lookahead ring extend its transitive fill lead by k: the effective pre-roll is the maximum declared latency among the anchor block's audible direct contributors, floored by the configured LookaheadRingConfig::preroll, so the ring warms output blocks k further ahead of the playhead; the mixed output blocks, their keys, and their windows are unchanged, so a primed ring drained in order stays byte-identical to the same scene rendered with latency() == Time::zero() and identical between worker_count == 0 and worker_count > 0; a scene declaring no latency extends the horizon by zero (no regression); latency in nested effect chains and dynamic latency (full PDC) stay deferred (extends 12-audio#lookahead-prepares-ahead-of-playhead)

A DeviceMonitor attached to a transport derives composition time from samples delivered: after the device consumes K frames at the device rate R, the transport position -- and the lock-free mastered-playhead snapshot the lookahead pump and video viewports on the same transport sample -- advance by exactly K/R; the Transport is mutated only on the single owner thread (never the RT callback), so its single-owner discipline is preserved; a host seek/rate change rebases the master's sample origin (pre-change frames do not re-advance) and reprimes the pump; a transport with no device monitor free-runs on the injected system clock byte-identically (no regression); a second device monitor on one transport is rejected

The device RT callback consumes only prepared, already-mixed blocks through the lookahead pump's drain and issues zero render_audio/mix_composition/pull_audio invocations on the callback thread; an unready block yields silence + an underrun count, never an inline mix; the bytes drained through the device path equal a direct mix_composition oracle for the same windows and are byte-identical between worker_count == 0 and worker_count > 0 (extends 12-audio#lookahead-prepares-ahead-of-playhead)

On a host seek/set_rate the device RT drain cursor is re-seated to the block covering the reprimed playhead and the pre-change working carry is dropped, so from the first post-change frame the bytes drained through the device path equal a fresh mix_composition oracle at the new playhead, byte-identical between worker_count == 0 and worker_count > 0 (extends 12-audio#device-callback-consumes-prepared-blocks-only)

Region damage invalidates a content's tiles by (content id, region) at every resident scale rung, not just the viewport rung; the caller injects the tile->content-space geometry, matching is revision- and achieved_time-agnostic (a sound over-approximation), non-intersecting tiles and other contents are spared, and the returned count equals the tiles removed

A revision bump fires no cache op: the fresh key simply misses while the prior-revision tile stays resident as the stale-revision fallback (evictions unchanged); the compositor reclaims the superseded tile only by an explicit drop_superseded, which is a removal not an eviction

Invalidating a pinned tile makes its key immediately unreachable to new lookups while deferring the surface drop to the pin's last release; resident bytes stay counted until then, and destroying the hold reclaims them

A composed-result tile keyed by an aggregate revision (a plain uint64 in the same revision slot) is invalidated by invalidate_content and drop_superseded identically to a leaf tile, so no cache-specific composite mechanism exists

An interactive request scale s is quantized to the smallest power-of-two rung 2^k >= s (prefer-downsample: rung >= needed scale), leaving a composite remainder s / 2^k in (0.5, 1.0] (<=1 octave), exactly 1.0 at power-of-two scales; coarser rungs are built by exact 2:1 Lanczos-3 half-band reduction in linear premultiplied working space

A visible tile whose fresh key (current revision, current rung) misses the cache becomes a BestEffort render request carrying the frame's Deadline value and the pinned StateHandle, targeting exactly the tile's local-space footprint at rung_scale; a qualifying cache hit issues no request

When a fresh exact tile is unavailable at plan time, the tile's display source is chosen in the order fresh -> stale-revision -> coarser-rung-rescaled -> checkerboard/transparent, taking the first available; a stale tile is reached by a deliberate prior-revision probe, a coarser tile by probing successively coarser rungs at the current revision

Planning an all-Static, warm-cache scene after advancing only the clock re-plans to all fresh cache hits and issues zero render requests, because Static tile keys omit achieved_time and are therefore clock-invariant

After a transaction commit, the housekeeper's between-transaction drain runs the reclamation queue to quiescence, so every slot released during that transaction is reclaimed before the next transaction begins and the arena's live count returns to its no-garbage baseline; disabling the between-transaction drain leaves those slots on the queue until an explicit quiesce

The housekeeper commits a checkpoint exactly when a configured trigger fires -- every Nth transaction, a tick past the interval with at least one intervening transaction, or an explicit host request -- and an automatic trigger on a scene with no transaction since the last checkpoint is skipped, so a still scene issues no checkpoint

The housekeeper exposes a wall-clock-free stats snapshot whose live-slot count reflects the arena's reachable slots after a drain and whose drain/commit event counters advance exactly once per drain/commit, giving a host memory panel an honest view of version-GC activity

Arena and SlotStore accounting accessors (bytes reserved, slots capacity/live, high water) are safe to read from a non-writer thread while the writer allocates and mints new size-class stores; the host memory-panel poll is race-free

The background housekeeping thread and the writer's between-transaction drain are serialized so exactly one thread drains the reclamation queue at a time; under concurrent RT-thread enqueues while a drain runs, every released slot is reclaimed exactly once and the arena returns to its no-garbage baseline, with no lost or double-freed slots
Requesting the background housekeeping thread to stop wakes it from its park, runs one final drain to quiescence, and joins cleanly, leaving no slot un-reclaimed at teardown

A Document's reclamation drain runs through its Housekeeper: after each transaction commit the queue is drained to quiescence, so every record the transaction released -- and every content state handle those records held -- is reclaimed before the next transaction begins, and the Model's live-slot count stays at its no-garbage baseline across an unbounded edit churn with no manual drain; a document left idle after its last edit is reclaimed by the background housekeeping thread, and the drain publishes the HAMT reclaim context, so interior edges are released rather than silently leaked

The background housekeeping thread never commits a checkpoint: on a workspace-backed Document driven concurrently by a writer, every Checkpointer commit is attributable to a writer-side trigger (the every-Nth-transaction cadence or an explicit host request), and the background thread's ticks advance only the drain counter

A workspace-backed Document commits a durable checkpoint exactly when a writer-side trigger fires -- every Nth transaction, or an explicit host request -- and an explicit request on a Document with no transaction since the last checkpoint skips the data msync, so an idle document issues no redundant durable writes; reopening the file resumes the last durable root, and an edit made after that checkpoint is not recovered

Content-state release routed on the drain thread while the writer concurrently binds editable contents releases each state handle exactly once and to its owning content, even when two contents' handles collide by value
Timed content renders a deterministic function of request time and reports the achieved_time it rendered
Static content ignores request time and reports nullopt achieved_time and nullopt time_extent so it adds no time dimension to the cache key

RenderResult.achieved_scale never exceeds request.scale; a degraded render (achieved_scale < request.scale) is never reported exact; and an Exact request is rendered faithfully (exact, at the requested scale)
Render produces no coverage outside bounds(): a request whose region lies entirely outside bounds() -- or, for Timed content, whose time lies outside time_extent() -- yields empty/transparent output

After an edit whose damage is confined to a rect D (a state transition from before to after), re-rendering any region disjoint from D returns bit-identical pixels; only pixels inside D may change (damage soundness)

Re-rendering a previously captured snapshot handle reproduces its pixels byte-for-byte, even after intervening renders under other handles: restoring a captured content state reproduces its render exactly. Skipped for Live content, which opts out

Declared facets are self-consistent: the description methods (bounds/stability/time_extent) are idempotent and mutually coherent -- Static content declares nullopt time_extent -- and snapshot sensitivity is optional (Live content need not be a deterministic function of its snapshot)

An operator's map_input_damage over-approximates: the true output footprint of an input-damage rect (the set of output pixels that change when the named input's rect changes) is contained in the reported mapped rect, never under-reported
When identity(request) returns input index N, the operator's rendered output for that request is bit-identical to input N's output rendered for the same request

The temporal prefetch ring enumerates exactly the upcoming temporal buckets in the playback direction within the horizon (base + dir*step*k for k >= 1 while |step*k| <= horizon); no bucket beyond the horizon and none in the reverse direction is enumerated, and a non-positive step or horizon yields an empty ring

Under budget pressure a Temporal-class entry is evicted after a Recent entry and before an Adjacent entry, pinning the victim-first priority order speculative < recent < temporal < adjacent < visible with exactly five classes

Applying a prefetch ring reclassifies already-resident ring members onto the ring's class (pan ring -> Adjacent, zoom rung -> Speculative, temporal ring -> Temporal) and returns exactly the absent ring keys as the want-list; the cache inserts and evicts nothing, so resident bytes and evictions are unchanged across the call

A tile render answered asynchronously is recorded pending rather than dropped; on completion it is inserted into the cache under Visible and emits Damage for its tile region, so a follow-up frame re-plans it as a fresh cache hit and composites it sharp.

A cache miss whose tile is already in flight -- recorded pending, unsettled and uncancelled -- issues no render request and allocates no surface at either dispatch site (the driver's tile loop, PullService::pull's per-covering-tile loop): requests_issued does not advance, requests_suppressed advances by one, and no second PendingTile is recorded. It is not treated as a hit: it contributes no pixels this pass and settles nothing, degrading exactly as the dispatch it deferred to would have, and the in-flight arrival's damage -- broadcast on the content's id to every consumer of the tile -- re-drives it.

A pending tile whose completion has been cancelled does NOT suppress re-dispatch: cancellation is advisory and does not settle, so a conformant content may honor it and settle via fail, which poll_refinements drops with no cache insert and no damage -- leaving nothing to re-drive a caller that had joined it. The next miss on that key dispatches its own render.

A pending tile whose render has SETTLED WITH A RESULT but has not yet been drained by poll_refinements suppresses re-dispatch exactly as an unsettled one does: its pixels exist but are not in the cache (the insert happens in poll_refinements, on the frame thread, and the interactive driver runs that poll AFTER the frame's plan pass), so a worker that settles between one frame's poll and the next frame's plan would otherwise be seen as a plain cache miss with nothing in flight -- and the plan would dispatch a SECOND render of a tile that has already finished, advancing requests_issued and discarding the finished result. The gate therefore reads the settlement's KIND, not its presence: settled-with-a-result joins, while settled-via-fail (dropped by poll with no insert and no damage) and already-taken do not, so a failed tile is still re-dispatched rather than stranded. The gate cannot stall on a settled entry -- the poll is unconditional and drains it on the very next pass, emitting the arrival damage that re-drives the tile.

An operator whose render leaves one or more input tiles unmet records them as that output tile's REFINEMENT WAVE, and while any of them is still in the pending queue the tile is not re-rendered: no content->render, no pull, no surface allocation, at either gate site (the driver's tile loop, PullService::pull's per-covering-tile loop). The chain is re-driven at most ONCE per wave, not once per independently-arriving input, so a nested chain over N async leaves costs one chain re-render rather than N. A deferred render bumps neither requests_issued nor operator_renders and bumps renders_coalesced instead, so the gate is provable by PRESENCE and not only by a number that failed to grow. The unmet set is transitive through a nested chain for free, because worker dispatch is leaf-only and an operator therefore renders inline on the driver thread: each level's wait names exactly the leaves beneath it. The wave is a SET, never a duration -- no timer, no wall clock, no frame counter -- and its only input is which TileKeys are still in the queue.

The wave gate opens on the very next plan of an operator tile once every input its last render left unmet has LEFT the pending queue -- by settling and being drained into the cache, by failing (dropped with no insert and no damage), or by being dropped -- and poll_refinements prunes the drained wait in the same pass. An input that is settled but not yet drained still holds the gate, because its pixels are not in the cache yet and a chain re-rendered against it would re-dispatch a render that has already completed; poll_refinements runs unconditionally every frame, before any re-plan, so the gate is held for at most the drain it is about to get. A cancelled-but-unsettled input also holds the gate -- the opposite of tile_in_flight, deliberately, because cancel is advisory and the render usually lands anyway; both of its futures (it lands, or it fails and is dropped) remove it from the queue and so open the gate. A wave that ends always releases what it gated.
A refinement poll with no settled arrivals emits zero damage, so a scene with no pending tiles and no other damage schedules no follow-up frame.

During a zoom gesture the compositor primes the next rung tiles for the visible region under PriorityClass::Speculative: resident next-rung tiles are reclassified Speculative and absent ones are returned as a want-list, the prime pass rendering and evicting nothing.

Re-anchoring the viewport across the well-conditioned-band threshold (rebuilding the camera relative to the new anchor via compose) leaves the device-space image of a probe point unchanged to within one double rounding (a few ULPs), the justified tolerance doc 04:66 states as "exact to within one double rounding".

Driving a synthetic chain nested N levels at ~1000x/level through repeated rebases keeps the composed anchor->device max_scale() inside the well-conditioned band at every level (structure-bounded, never diverging with depth), whereas viewing the deepest node through a single non-rebased root camera drives max_scale() astronomically outside the band.

A nested-composition subtree whose composed on-screen extent drops below the sub-pixel threshold is culled by the viewport-outward walk without descending, so no leaf under it is visited and it emits zero render requests.

A scripted zoom-in past the well-conditioned-band threshold followed by a zoom-out re-anchors the viewport outward along the runtime-held anchor path: the camera is rebuilt by inverting the stored descent edge (reanchor_camera(camera, edge.inverse())), the composed device-space image of a probe point is preserved across the switch to within one double rounding, and the round-trip restores the original (anchor, matrix).

Every re-anchor (zoom in or out) is surfaced from HostViewport::step as Reanchor{occurred=true, from, to} with the correct old/new anchor ids, and a frame with no re-anchor surfaces occurred==false; the reanchor_events() counter equals the scripted re-anchor count over a zoom sequence.

The compositor exposes wall-clock-free debug counters -- render requests issued, cache hits/misses, tiles composited -- as caller-owned std::uint64_t fields, so a still-scene warm-cache re-plan is observable as requests_issued == 0 with all cache hits, not as a timing.

Model damage (content, region, range) maps through the per-viewport camera to device dirty rects: damage outside the displayed instant or the viewport contributes none, structural infinite damage maps to the full viewport, and empty damage yields an empty dirty region -- no damage, no work

A clock advance emits temporal damage only for visible non-Static layers; an advance over an all-Static scene emits zero damage, so a still scene during playback schedules no frame and the interactive driver issues zero renders and zero composites

A Timed layer's requested time is quantized to the content's native grid via quantize_time before the cache lookup, so a clock advance that stays within one native frame period re-plans to all-fresh cache hits and issues zero renders and zero composites; an advance across a native-frame-period boundary renders exactly once

A content's render-free quantize_time(t), when present, equals the achieved_time a render at t reports and is idempotent, so the compositor may key a tile at the native instant before rendering and the render lands on that key

On a cache miss the compositor keys a Timed tile at the achieved_time its render reports (equal to the pre-quantized plan key); a direct TileCache lookup at any instant within the same native frame period returns that stored surface (a hit that increments hits(), not misses()), while an instant across a native-frame boundary keys a distinct entry (a miss); so coalescing serves the exact rendered frame and reuse is sound under seek, and a Timed content whose render lands off its own quantize_time grid is caught at the insert site rather than serving a wrong frame

A worker pool with zero workers runs every submitted render on the calling thread and settles its RenderCompletion on that thread, producing pixels and a settlement byte-identical to running the same RenderRequest inline; the request and completion structure is preserved so real workers change performance, not results.

For content declaring render_thread_safe() == false the pool renders at most one request for that content at a time (a per-content FIFO), while thread-safe content renders concurrently; under many interleaved concurrent submissions the serialized content's in-flight render count never exceeds one and every submission settles exactly once.

Requesting the worker pool to stop wakes every parked worker, lets in-flight renders finish, refuses new submissions, and joins all workers cleanly with no hang and no lost or double-settled completion.

Worker dispatch is leaf-only: every worker-backed RenderDispatch in the tree is obtained from runtime's single worker_backed_dispatch(pool) helper, which submits a content to the pool only when it is a leaf (empty inputs()) and renders an operator content (non-empty inputs() -- fade, crossfade, nested; doc 13) inline on the calling driver thread, because an operator's render re-enters the PullService whose cache probe/insert and descent-depth accounting are render-thread-confined. Driven with worker_count > 0 through both the offline parallel-exact path and the interactive frame loop over a scene holding all three operator kinds, every operator render is observed on the driver thread and none on any worker, at least one leaf render is observed off-thread, the frames are byte-identical to the worker_count == 0 run, and the suite is TSan-clean; a grep-lint keeps the helper the only submission site.

The interactive driver ships with a NON-ZERO worker count (default_interactive_pool_config(), a runtime policy derived from the machine's hardware concurrency and capped, never a fixed number), so a leaf miss renders on a worker and not on the frame thread. This is what makes the Step 4 deadline promise keepable: at worker_count == 0 the pool is the degenerate inline executor and submit IS the render, so the frame thread sits inside a slow leaf's render while the deadline passes and reaches the deadline park only after every miss has been rendered to completion -- nothing left to degrade to, nothing in flight to retain, and nothing to cancel. Over one scene and one already-expired injected clock, differing only in the pool config: at WorkerPoolConfig{} the leaf's render is observed on the frame thread and the frame degrades nothing, retains nothing and cancels nothing; at the shipped default the leaf's render is observed on a worker thread and the frame returns with the deadline expired, having left the still-wanted in-flight BestEffort render IN FLIGHT (tiles_retained grows, tiles_cancelled does not) and composited the previous revision's tile as the degraded fallback -- the degrade being the park's doing, not the cancel's -- then refines to the sharp tile when the arrival settles on a later frame. The default is >= 1 and <= k_max_interactive_workers on any machine, and an explicit WorkerPoolConfig{} still selects the thread-free inline executor.

Several InteractiveRenderers may share one WorkerPool, and a renderer parked in the pool's completion wait observes only its OWN settles: the pool keeps the settle counter and each parking thread keeps its own drain cursor, so a sibling's completion may WAKE a parked renderer (the wake is a broadcast, because poke() is the contract-facing async handle and its caller knows nothing about renderers) but can never be CONSUMED by it. Two independent cursors over one pool both observe a single settle, and a settle that occurs after one cursor has drained is still observed by a cursor that drained earlier and by a cursor that has never parked at all; a cursor already at the head of the counter parks and times out, so the cursors track something real rather than always answering true. At the driver: with a sibling renderer parking against an already-expired clock as fast as it can loop, a renderer whose leaves all settle promptly still reaches quiescence with zero deadline expiries, zero cancelled tiles, zero degraded composites and no unearned follow-up frame -- because a wake it loses is the only thing that could make it expire, and it would then cancel or degrade tiles sitting finished in its own RefinementQueue

A renderer borrowing a WorkerPool drains only its OWN submissions out of it when it dies, and the pool and its siblings survive. ~InteractiveRenderer calls WorkerPool::drain_owner(this), which purges the owner's not-yet-started tasks from the ready queue and from every per-content parked FIFO (releasing any serialization gate a purged task was holding, so the content is not stranded), then blocks until every one of its STARTED tasks has returned from content->render -- waiting on no-longer-outstanding rather than on settled, which terminates unconditionally because a cancelled task still runs and content that settles off-thread may never settle. It does NOT call request_stop (terminal and pool-global: one viewport closing would strand every sibling's renders unsettled forever) and does not cancel, purge or wait for a sibling's work. Driven through the real frame loop: a renderer destroyed while a worker is inside its leaf's render does not return from its destructor until that render has returned (the render's last act writes into a PendingTile surface the destructor is about to free, so an early return is an ASan/TSan use-after-free, not a lucky pass); a renderer destroyed with M never-started tasks queued has exactly M counted by tasks_dropped() and none of them run; and through both, a sibling sharing the pool keeps its in-flight renders, converges afterwards, and still gets a usable pool

The interactive frame loop never blocks past its frame deadline: it parks for async arrivals only until the deadline and then RETURNS, compositing the best-available fallback in the placeholder preference order rather than waiting, and cancelling (via RenderCompletion::cancel) the pending renders it no longer wants. The bound is the park, not the cancel: the frame returns at the deadline whether or not anything is cancelled. The sharp result refines on a follow-up frame, and a failed or cancelled render degrades to the placeholder policy, never thrown through the loop

On deadline expiry the interactive frame's sweep cancels ONLY the pending tiles the frame no longer wants: a tile whose key is absent from the frame's visible footprint (every TileKey a surviving visible layer covers over the whole viewport at the chosen rung and revision, plus every key the frame's pulls named) and absent from the unmet inputs of any live OperatorWait whose output is itself wanted. A still-wanted unsettled render is left in flight, uncancelled -- tiles_retained and tiles_cancelled together partition the unsettled entries at expiry. Wantedness is derived from VISIBILITY, not from what the frame planned: a partial repaint plans a strict subset of what it wants, and a plan-derived set would cancel a visible, still-missing tile whose region merely was not dirty -- which, since cancellation is advisory and a conformant content may honor it and fail (a failed arrival being dropped with no insert and no damage), would strand that tile behind a placeholder with nothing left to re-plan it. Superseded-by-revision and no-longer-visible both fall out of full TileKey equality: a revision bump, a pan, a zoom to another rung, a clock advance, or a layer culled out of its time span each leave the old key out of the footprint

A pending tile the deadline sweep retained across an expiry suppresses its own re-dispatch on the following frame that re-plans it: over a two-frame sequence whose second frame re-plans every tile the first dispatched while their renders are still in flight, requests_issued() equals the number of distinct TileKeys the sequence needs (the inline oracle's count) and does not grow across the boundary, requests_suppressed() does grow (the positive witness that the cross-frame guard fired, not merely that a number failed to move), tiles_retained() > 0, tiles_cancelled() == 0, no second surface is allocated, and the quiesced image is byte-identical to the inline oracle's. This is what makes the in-flight join a cross-frame mechanism: a blanket cancel on expiry disqualifies every entry that survives a frame boundary from suppression (tile_in_flight refuses to trust a cancelled entry), so cross-frame dedup provably never fires

An input tile named unmet by a live OperatorWait whose output the frame still wants is NOT cancelled by the deadline sweep, even though the refinement wave gate meant the operator never rendered and so never re-pulled it this frame -- so its wave can still end. The guard is symmetric: a wait whose output the frame no longer wants (panned away, revision-bumped, culled out of its span) retains nothing, and its inputs are cancelled like any other unwanted pending, which keeps the clause from degenerating into "anything ever waited on is forever wanted" and keeps tiles_cancelled a live path

An interactive frame with no model damage, no clock-advance damage (all-Static or a sub-native-frame advance), and no settled pending refinements collects empty damage, plans nothing, and issues zero render requests and zero composites through the assembled loop, scheduling no follow-up frame (requests_issued/composites/follow_up_frames deltas all 0)
A HostViewport with no pending damage, no owed follow-up, and no scene motion issues zero render_frame invocations across repeated idle steps (a still scene costs nothing); frames_issued() stays 0.

render_frame_interactive optionally surfaces the per-visible-layer LayerTilePlans it composited (in composite order, only when the caller supplies a sink; the null default is byte-identical to no sink); the interactive loop's speculation step drives prime_prefetch from those exposed plans rather than re-planning, so it issues zero render requests and zero composites and leaves requests_issued/composites unchanged from the plan pass, while reclassifying the resident pan/zoom prefetch-ring tiles

Compositing the same damage-gated frame twice onto the same caller-persisted target is a no-op: the frame clears its device repaint region before re-compositing it and clips every composite onto the target to that same region, so a translucent layer's source-over contribution lands exactly once no matter how many times the gated frame is re-run -- decisively not accumulated per pass

An interactive sequence driven to quiescence through damage-gated refine frames (at any worker_count) lands on pixels byte-identical to a single un-gated full pass over the same scene from the same warm cache: a gated frame's repaint region is exactly what one full pass would have put there

A damage-gated frame writes no pixel outside its device repaint region: every pixel of the caller-persisted target outside that region is byte-identical to what the previous frame left there, so "repaint everything" is not the fix

The frame's device repaint region is a set of pairwise-disjoint, integer-aligned rects inside the viewport whose union is exactly the union of the (viewport-clipped, rounded-out) device dirty rects: every damaged device pixel lies in exactly one repaint rect -- so it is cleared once and repainted once -- and no undamaged pixel outside that union lies in any of them. The bounding box of the set is repaint_region, and a decomposition exceeding k_max_repaint_rects falls back to exactly that bounding box (the shipped byte-exact behavior, whose union is a superset, so no damage is ever missed)

A damage-gated frame that repaints the disjoint rect set is byte-identical to the same frame forced through the bounding box of the same damage, on a translucent scene from the same warm cache and the same persisted target -- while compositing strictly fewer tiles: the gap pixels the bounding box repaints are undamaged, so by the persisted-target contract they already hold precisely what it repaints into them, and skipping them is byte-free (no existing golden needs a new baseline)

A gated frame whose damage is two far-apart rects composites only the tiles its disjoint repaint rects overlap -- strictly fewer than the bounding-box repaint of the same damage, which composites every tile between them -- and clears once per repaint rect rather than once over the box, while issuing no additional render requests: a tile straddling two repaint rects is planned once per rect, merged to a single plan entry by tile coord, rendered once, and composited once per rect, each composite clipped to its own rect, so the pixels are still written exactly once; a layer meeting no repaint rect is not planned, not composited, and not exposed in visible_plans

A raster paint stroke touching tile set T produces a captured state that shares every tile outside T with the prior state by refcount (no copy); the paint allocates exactly |T| new level-0 blobs plus the mip blobs geometrically above T, capture copies O(|T|) index slots, and the emitted damage equals the touched tile set T

The runtime routes an editable content's state onto the live Model/Journal on instantiation (Document::add_content, through the contract Editable facet -- no manual set_*_sink call and no concrete-kind dependency) and tears it down on release, for ANY NUMBER of editable contents in one document: each one's edits are journaled and undoable through the document's single Journal, its captured versions are priced into the journal budget by the registered StateCostFn, a published version's handle is retained exactly once, a pinned version's tile blobs survive any later edit, and reclaiming its record on document teardown fires release exactly once per retain so every version refcount returns to 0 -- per content, independently; a non-editable content registers nothing and keeps the byte-identical inert path

Every content-state seam names its owning ObjectId (StateRefSink::retain/release, StateCostFn::cost, RestoreSink::on_restore), and a document holding N editable contents dispatches each retain, release, cost and restore through its single per-Document sink trio to the content that owns the handle -- and to no other -- even when the contents hold colliding StateHandles (each content's slots are numbered in its own store); the sink trio is registered on the Model/Journal exactly once, at attach, regardless of N, and a state call naming an ObjectId with no routing row is counted on a behavioral counter that is zero across the suite

Releasing one content's state version never touches another content's: with two rasters in one document at colliding version slots, reclaiming one's record frees only that content's tile blobs, while the other's identically-valued handle stays retained and fully resolvable to its own pixels (a raster StateHandle transitively owns its tile blobs through BigBlockPool, so a release routed by handle alone would free the wrong content's pixels)

After org.arbc.raster::paint, every mip rung of the resulting tile table is byte-identical to a full rebuild of the pyramid from the painted level-0 pixels: the incremental recompute dilates the propagated region by the decimation kernel's support at each rung, so no stale filtered pixel survives near a painted tile's boundary

A raster dab composites color over the destination at a caller-supplied per-pixel coverage in premultiplied linear working floats (out = color*a + dst*(1 - color[3]*a), per-channel non-negative-clamped, no libm), not a replace; a full-coverage opaque dab reduces to the prior REPLACE byte-for-byte, so the CoW/mip/coalescing/damage claims hold unchanged, while a partial-coverage or partial-opacity dab blends against the destination exactly once per covered pixel

An operator's aggregate revision changes iff some reachable input's revision contribution changes; the fold is order-independent (permuting inputs() yields the same value) and visits each reachable node exactly once, so a shared diamond input is folded once and a cyclic inputs() graph still terminates

Damage on an input propagates to every visible operator that reaches it through inputs(), the output rect being the composed map_input_damage mapping (over-approximating / covering); an operator that does not reach the damaged input receives none

A structural walk (aggregate-revision fold, damage route) over a cyclic inputs() graph terminates with each node visited once; a planning descent (identity chain) that exceeds the per-request recursion-depth budget selects the placeholder and reports one diagnostic naming the offending content, without unbounded recursion

Rendering an org.arbc.nested content over a child composition produces pixels byte-identical to compositing that child's layers directly at top level under the equivalent viewport (region-to-surface camera, same target), across native / 0.5x / 0.25x -- the rendering-is-recursion identity

A static nested subtree's composed-result tile survives a clock advance and an unrelated edit (zero dispatched renders, requests_issued delta 0), being keyed by the child's aggregate revision; an edit deep in a shared reachable child bumps that aggregate revision and re-renders exactly the spine (requests_issued delta 1)

An org.arbc.nested content's bounds()/stability()/time_extent() recompute only when the child's aggregate revision changes: repeated queries at a stable revision recompute zero times, and a revision bump re-keys the memo exactly once

Every pull an org.arbc.nested content issues for a child layer carries the outer request's snapshot StateHandle, deadline, and exactness verbatim -- never reset or sub-budgeted per level -- and a depth-1 nested scene issues exactly one pull per visible child layer, so the recursive case costs what the equivalent flat scene costs

The runtime attaches an org.arbc.nested content's four render-time services (the driver's live PullService and Backend, an id->Content* resolver, and the driver's PINNED DocRoot -- never a freshly-pinned one) when a RENDER DRIVER binds a document's content graph -- the interactive frame loop per frame, the offline SequenceRenderer and the export ExportMonitor over theirs -- with no manual attach call and no concrete-kind dependency outside the single thunk TU; so a nested scene rendered through SequenceRenderer, or through a Document-bound interactive viewport, is byte-identical to the same scene attached by hand, and a nested-of-tones scene drained through ExportMonitor is byte-identical to the direct-mix oracle instead of silent. The walk attaches a content before enumerating its inputs(), so a content reachable only through a nested boundary (a fade in the child composition) binds too; and the binding tears down on scope release, clearing every borrowed pointer exactly once per attach, leaving the pinned snapshot alive for as long as any content it was injected into

A visible operator layer whose identity(request) returns an input index is planned with zero operator renders and creates no operator-output cache entry; the operator_renders counter delta is 0, while a non-identity operator layer that is inline-rendered records exactly one

A PullService::pull whose input tile is resident under the input's identity and aggregate revision completes done synchronously from the cache and dispatches no render (dispatch-count delta 0, requests_issued delta 0); a pull whose fresh key misses dispatches exactly one render carrying the request unchanged, inserts the result tile under the input's identity, and settles done from it

The render a pull dispatches for a cache miss carries the pull request's snapshot StateHandle and deadline value verbatim -- neither reset nor recomputed -- and each recursive pull propagates the same snapshot token, so every visit to a node within one frame sees one revision set

A PullService::pull whose covering tile is already in flight -- pending, unsettled and uncancelled -- dispatches nothing for that tile and delivers nothing: the cache is still probed first and the pending set only on a miss, so the rule narrows pull-is-cache-first rather than replacing it. The joined tile counts as async, so the region is left unsettled and inexact (TRANSIENT -- something more is coming for this revision, from the dispatch it deferred to), the operator degrades this pass, and the in-flight arrival's damage re-drives every consumer of that input, including the pull that never registered with it

A PullService::pull for an operator's transient output tile -- resident under its exact key but flagged inexact, so it is not a hit -- whose recorded unmet inputs are still pending DELIVERS the resident transient tile into the caller's target, dispatches no render, and leaves the region unsettled and inexact (TRANSIENT: more is genuinely coming, from the wave it is waiting on), so the parent degrades this pass exactly as it did before. Unlike the in-flight join it must deliver, because the caller is about to composite this tile now and an undelivered target is a transparent hole in its placeholder; the caller also inherits the child's outstanding input keys as its own unmet set, so both gates open on the same wave. The placeholder itself stays inexact -- the gate sits between the miss and the render, never in the hit predicate.

PullService::pull writes the pulled input's pixels into the caller's request.target -- the visual analog of pull_audio delivering into AudioRequest.target -- on both the resident cache-hit path and the synchronous miss path, honoring the request's region and scale, and dispatches no additional render to do so

A PullService::pull whose request.region spans more than one tile fills the caller's request.target across every covering tile of tiles_covering(rung, region): each covering tile is independently cache-probed and, on a resident exact-fresh hit, composited into target under its own tile->region affine, or, on a miss, rendered into a cache-owned surface, composited into target, and inserted -- the same per-tile sequence render_frame_interactive runs, with delivery added; the caller's done settles exactly once from the aggregate: exact iff every covering tile is exact at the selected rung, with the uniform rung scale and achieved_time; any covering tile answering asynchronously leaves done unsettled so the operator degrades this frame and each async tile's arrival re-drives it; any covering tile failing inline degrades the whole region to the placeholder

A render dispatched by PullService::pull has its target surface retained until the render settles; a caller's RenderCompletion::cancel() never frees a surface an in-flight worker is still writing, and a pull with no async reap sink dispatches synchronously so its surface is consumed before pull returns

org.arbc.fade scales its input's premultiplied RGBA (visual) and its samples (audio) by the same envelope evaluated at the request time; a fully-open envelope is a no-op and a fully-closed envelope yields transparent / silent output
org.arbc.fade reports Timed stability on both facets even when its input is Static, because its envelope depends on time
org.arbc.fade identity() returns its single input iff the envelope evaluates to exactly 1.0 at the request time, and nullopt otherwise

A visible operator obtains every input's pixels and samples only through PullService::pull / PullService::pull_audio and never calls input->render() / render_audio() directly: driven over poison-leaf inputs through a recording PullService the inputs' direct-render flags stay false while the service records at least one pull per input, and driven through the live PullServiceImpl bound to CompositorCounters every input render/audio dispatch the operator provokes equals one the service issued (each input's observed render count equals requests_issued and its render_audio count equals audio_dispatches), so the operator never bypasses the core's caching / scheduling / snapshot / budget path

The runtime attaches a live PullService/Backend onto an operator content (org.arbc.fade) at instantiation so its render()/render_audio() runs with no manual attach, and tears the binding down on release so no borrowed service is dereferenced after; a document containing a fade layer rendered through the real offline/export driver produces bytes identical to the manually-attached golden and issues its input pulls only through the live PullServiceImpl

The runtime attaches a live PullService/Backend onto a two-input operator content (org.arbc.crossfade) at instantiation so its render()/render_audio() runs with no manual attach, and tears the binding down on release so no borrowed service is dereferenced after; a document containing a crossfade rendered through the real offline/export driver produces bytes identical to the manually-attached golden and issues both inputs' pulls only through the live PullServiceImpl

org.arbc.crossfade combines its two inputs across both facets by a single time-evaluated position w(t): the visual facet is a source-over of input 1 over input 0 at opacity w (the textbook linear dissolve in0*(1-w)+in1*w with full alpha for opaque inputs), and the audio facet is a per-frame complementary-weight additive mix s0*(1-w)+s1*w; at w==0 the output is byte-identical to input 0 and at w==1 to input 1 on both facets

org.arbc.crossfade's bounds(), time_extent(), and audio_extent() are each the bounding union of its two inputs' respective extents (min start / max end, min corner / max corner) -- not fade's single-input pass-through and never an intersection
org.arbc.crossfade reports Timed stability on both facets even when both inputs are Static, because its position w(t) depends on time

org.arbc.crossfade identity() returns input 0 iff w(request.time)==0.0 exactly and input 1 iff w==1.0 exactly, and nullopt in the interior; this is sound because render() at each endpoint pulls the corresponding input straight through, so the compositor serving that input's cached tiles is bit-identical to a render

In the offline/export pull configuration each operator input child receives a distinct, stable cache identity -- distinct from every model ObjectId in the document (drawn from a reserved identity namespace the model allocator never issues) and from every sibling child, keyed by pointer identity so a shared child is shared by every consumer -- so two same-stability inputs of one operator cache under different keys and render byte-exact

A visible operator layer whose identity(request) returns input N is composited into the offline frame with input N's pixels: the frame driver serves input N's cached tiles into the layer's tile footprint through PullService::pull (every covering tile, cache-first, delivered via deliver_tile) and composites them at the layer's device affine and opacity -- byte-identical to input N rendered directly -- while issuing zero operator renders and creating no operator-output cache entry (the delivery counterpart of 13-effects-as-operators#identity-plan-issues-no-operator-render)

Binding the document's content graph is the render driver's obligation and every driver discharges it, so no host attaches an operator by hand: the interactive frame loop binds once per frame that actually renders, against that frame's own frame-local PullService, exactly as the offline and export drivers bind against theirs -- and the binding is torn down with the frame that borrowed the service it holds, never cached across frames. So an operator layer that is NOT an identity endpoint -- a fade at an interior envelope, a crossfade at an interior weight, a nesting, all of which run their own render() and pull their inputs through the service they received at attach -- composites the same pixels under an interactive deadline as it does in an exact export, byte for byte; and a still scene, which early-outs ahead of the pull service, binds zero times

The degraded placeholder an operator renders when a pull leaves its completion unsettled -- the pull was DISPATCHED to a worker, so the real answer is still coming -- is reported INEXACT on both facets (RenderResult::exact == false, AudioResult::exact == false), so a caller never caches it as a final answer and the dispatched render's arrival re-drives the operator; a pull that FAILED or exceeded its budget is a FINAL placeholder for this revision and is reported exact. Driven per operator kind (fade, crossfade, nested) and per facet with a pull double that leaves the completion unsettled: every deferring pass yields a transparent/silent placeholder reported exact == false, every failing pass yields the same placeholder reported exact == true, an aggregating operator ANDs the transient bits with its inputs' own exactness (a crossfade whose input answers inexactly, or at a lower achieved_rate, reports the same), and a crossfade still issues BOTH input pulls on a pass where the first one deferred

A chain of per-edge time maps composed to a leaf is evaluated in exact rational arithmetic and converted to an integer-flick instant exactly once, at the leaf; the composed result equals the mathematically exact affine composition (including reverse playback under a negative rate) and is independent of how the chain is grouped, so precision does not degrade with depth.

Rational rates compose as exact reduced rationals with no floating-point error across deep pathological stacks (e.g. repeated 24000/1001 and 1/2 nesting stays exact and in lowest terms), and a composition that would overflow the integer width even after reduction returns a TimeError value rather than wrapping or aborting.

A layer with span [in, out) in parent time is present iff in <= parent_time < out (half-open, out excluded); the default all() span is always present so a still is the degenerate case, and a degenerate span (out <= in) is present at no instant.

A layer instance carries a span and a time_map beside its spatial transform; a layer created with no temporal placement has span == TimeRange::all() and an identity time map (in=0, rate=1/1, offset=0), so a still is the degenerate case of a timed layer, not a special record.

Advancing a playing transport by real elapsed delta at rate r moves the playhead by exactly round_ties_even(delta*r) flicks; a negative rate steps backward by the same magnitude (sign-symmetric); a realistic rate (e.g. 24000/1001, 1/2) is exact; a pathological rate that overflows the flick width returns a TimeError value and leaves the playhead unchanged, never wrapping.

With half-open loop [in, out) set, a forward advance reaching or passing out re-enters in [in, out) via true modulo (including an advance longer than one loop length); a reverse advance past in wraps symmetrically at in; a transport with no loop advances unbounded; and a degenerate loop (out <= in) applies no wrap.

A paused transport's advance is a no-op (playhead unchanged, delta zero) and play() resumes at the pre-pause rate; seek(t) sets the playhead to exactly t regardless of loop bounds or pause state (scrub-anywhere).
Two transports over one document are independent value state: advancing or seeking one never mutates the other, and sampling the shared document through each yields that transport's own instant.

derive_playback_hint yields direction +1 for a positive rate and -1 for a negative rate (the sign of the rate, with no stored direction); both a paused transport and a zero rate yield the empty hint (direction 0, horizon 0): no motion, no pre-roll, an empty temporal ring.

derive_playback_hint's horizon equals |rate| times the real-time lookahead, computed in exact rational arithmetic with one ties-to-even leaf rounding (reusing the transport's TimeMap scaling); doubling the rate doubles the content-time horizon, reverse (rate < 0) gives the same magnitude as forward, and a pathological rate that overflows the flick width returns a TimeError value rather than wrapping.
