The default Content::playback_hint is a void no-op: a scene of hint-ignoring content renders byte-identically and issues zero extra renders whether or not a hint is delivered, so the advisory hint changes no pixels and no cache correctness.

drive_playback_prefetch fans the derived hint out to the frame's participating Timed content and, for each visible Timed anchor key, reclassifies exactly the resident temporal-neighbour tiles (ring intersect resident, bounded by the derived horizon) onto PriorityClass::Temporal and returns the absent members as the want-list, while resident_bytes() and evictions() are unchanged and a Static-only anchor builds an empty ring.

An org.arbc.imageseq content asked for a sub-frame time reports achieved_time == quantize_time(t) == the nearest native source-frame instant (floor to the native grid, exact rational with one ties-to-even leaf rounding, clamped to the frame range); quantize_time is idempotent; and two distinct times inside one native frame interval render byte-identical pixels.

The interactive compositor evaluates each present layer's time_map at the frame's composition time and requests its content at the resulting content-local time local = (composition_time - in) * rate + offset (doc 11:66-71, 122-124): a forward rate != 1 with non-zero in/offset scales and shifts the requested instant, and a negative rate reverses it (advancing composition time requests earlier content-local time), so the tile key and miss RenderRequest carry the retimed instant, not the raw composition time; the identity default map requests composition time unchanged.

Across a nested visual boundary the compositor remaps the child sub-request's time through that edge's time_map (per edge, never accumulated), so a non-identity rate/offset selects a decisively different child instant, not the parent time forwarded as identity; the identity default map forwards the parent instant unchanged.

A shared-library plugin dlopened and invoked through extern "C" arbc_plugin_register(Registry&) populates the Registry with its kind id and yields a factory that constructs a working Content; registration and factory errors are values (a duplicate/empty id or a missing source), never exceptions across the boundary.

The stb-class decode dependency resolves only in arbc-plugin-imageseq (its impl archive and loadable module) and no codec symbol appears in libarbc's exported symbols / link line, so a codec never enters an embedder's link line (the codec-line containment proof).

An offline sequence renderer pins one document revision at construction and renders every frame of the sequence against it, so a writer thread committing new revisions during the export changes no exported frame; frame N never observes frame N+1's edits and the whole sequence is revision-consistent

An offline frame renders every visible cache miss to completion with no deadline and composites only fresh, exact-scale, current-revision tiles; it never substitutes a stale, coarser, or placeholder tile regardless of how long a render takes, so the exported pixels are the exact result and the degraded-composites counter reads zero

A sequence render over a half-open time range at an output rate samples the composition at exactly the output frame instants (integer flicks), threading each as composition time; per-frame span-cull, time-map evaluation, and quantize_time coalescing are applied, so the sequence is deterministic and byte-reproducible and a Timed layer yields its native frame at each output instant

A Content that does not override audio() returns nullptr (audio-less: facet discovery finds no audio and it costs the audio engine nothing); a Content that exposes an AudioFacet returns it by pointer identity across repeated calls and answers audio_extent()/audio_stability()/latency() (latency defaults to Time::zero()), with render_audio settling exactly once -- inline or via the shared one-shot completion, the audio twin of the render one-code-path

A Content's AudioFacet is self-consistent: audio_extent()/audio_stability()/latency() are idempotent and mutually coherent (Static audio declares nullopt audio_extent, Timed audio declares a present audio_extent, latency() is stable); a render_audio window lying entirely outside audio_extent() yields an all-zero (silent) block; and for Static/Timed audio the target samples are a deterministic, block-continuous function of (snapshot, window, rate, layout) -- two requests with equal inputs settle to bit-identical samples, and splitting a window at an interior frame boundary then concatenating the two sub-renders is bit-identical to the single-window render. Discovered via Content::audio() and skipped for audio-less (nullptr) and Live content

A DeviceMonitor whose device rate exceeds the working rate resamples the drained working-rate mix to the device rate with the shipped polyphase kernel (resample_audio's coefficient table + exact phase math, no second algorithm): the device-delivered bytes equal a single whole-stream resample_audio reconstruction of the mix_composition oracle over the same window, byte-exact with no tolerance and continuous across block/callback boundaries (no per-block phase restart), byte-identical between worker_count == 0 and worker_count > 0; the mastered clock still advances by delivered_device_frames / device_rate; a seek/rate change flushes the resampler phase + history so post-flush output restarts byte-exact; a device rate equal to the working rate keeps the 1:1 drain unchanged, and a device rate below the working rate is rejected at construction (extends 12-audio#device-callback-consumes-prepared-blocks-only)

A DeviceMonitor whose device rate is below the working rate decimates the drained working-rate mix to the device rate with the same polyphase machinery extended by a ratio-scaled widened lowpass cut at the device Nyquist (the same windowed-sinc prototype and exact-integer phase math, coefficients generated off the RT thread at construction, no second algorithm): the anti-alias lowpass suppresses content above the device Nyquist rather than folding it into the audible band; the device-delivered bytes equal a single whole-stream resample_audio decimation of the mix_composition oracle over the same window, byte-exact with no tolerance and continuous across block/callback boundaries (no per-block phase restart), byte-identical between worker_count == 0 and worker_count > 0; the mastered clock still advances by delivered_device_frames / device_rate; a seek/rate change flushes the resampler and realigns the drain so post-flush output restarts byte-exact; the matched-rate 1:1 drain and the above-working upsample path are unchanged (extends 12-audio#device-edge-resamples-working-to-device)

Every function on the device RT callback chain -- the fill, the ring->callback drain, the edge format/rate conversion -- is [[clang::nonblocking]]-clean: executing it under an armed RtScope performs zero heap allocation, zero lock acquisition, and zero refcount operation, and RealtimeSanitizer reports no violation; the check is build-failing, not a counter. The lookahead ring->callback handoff is lock-free (a per-slot seqlock-published, pre-allocated ring; the former pump-drain mutex is removed), so the annotated graph carries no lock; verified across the shipped device-monitor scenarios (matched-rate drain, up/down device-edge SRC, post-seek realign, and the starved underrun/silence branch) with worker_count 0, and the byte-exact drain/lookahead goldens are unchanged (structural upgrade of 12-audio#device-callback-consumes-prepared-blocks-only)

The export monitor tiles a half-open time range into contiguous working-rate blocks and mixes each through mix_composition in Exact mode against one pinned revision; the concatenated block output is byte-identical to mix_composition called once per window directly and independent of the block size, every block reports exact == true and achieved_rate == working_rate for a rate-honoring composition (folding min/conjunction honestly for a below-rate contributor), facet-less/silent layers issue zero pull_audio dispatches, and a re-run produces byte-identical output -- the offline sample-exact drive of the mix core

The export monitor pins one document revision at construction and mixes every block of the range against it, so a writer thread committing new revisions during the export changes no exported block; every block reads DocRoot::revision() == R and the export equals the single-revision golden -- the audio twin of 02-architecture#offline-sequence-pins-single-revision

An ExportMonitor asked for a container output rate different from the composition working rate converts the working-rate mix to that output rate at the export edge with the same shipped band-limited polyphase StreamingResampler the device edge uses (up-sampling through the frozen input-Nyquist table for output_rate > working_rate; the ratio-scaled widened lowpass cut at the output Nyquist for output_rate < working_rate; no second algorithm): the concatenated container-rate export output is byte-exact against a single whole-stream resample_audio of the whole-range working mix over the same range, with no tolerance and continuous across export block boundaries (no per-block phase restart); every block still mixes against the one pinned revision; a requested output rate equal to the working rate keeps the existing 1:1 export path unchanged with zero resampler engagement (extends 12-audio#device-edge-resamples-working-to-device, 12-audio#device-edge-decimates-working-to-device)

In Spatial mode (an AudioRequest carrying a Spatialization context) a layer's contribution is attenuated by clamp(max_scale(layer.transform), 0, 1) applied once per edge, so a contributor's net attenuation composes multiplicatively down its nesting chain to its full composed scale (<= 1, amplification capped at unity); the camera's own uniform scale-attenuation post-scales the root mix once; a Flat request (no spatial context) is byte-identical to the mix-engine Flat path

In Spatial mode a layer is panned by a square-root constant-power law (t = (p+1)/2, gL = sqrt(1-t), gR = sqrt(t)) from its composed viewport x-position normalized about viewport_w/2 and clamped -- center is equal power, full-left is L-only, full-right is R-only -- byte-exact via IEEE sqrt; a child is mono-collapsed to a point source before placement, so pan collapses across nesting boundaries, and mono output applies attenuation only

In Spatial mode a contributor whose accumulated attenuation times its edge attenuation falls below the sub-audible threshold (default 2^-12, carried in the context) contributes nothing and is not descended, in both the L4 mix_layer and the L3 NestedContent::mix_child_layer walk sites, so a Droste embedding terminates the recursion at a finite depth strictly inside the doc-05 depth budget; the equivalent Flat scene terminates only at the depth budget (dispatching max_depth times)

In Spatial mode the LookaheadRing warming descent (LookaheadRing::descend) applies the same sub-audible cull as the mix walk -- a contributor whose accumulated attenuation times its edge attenuation (clamp(max_scale(transform),0,1)) falls below the context's sub_audible threshold is neither warmed nor descended -- so a Spatial Droste/shrinking-zoom scene warms a finite contributor closure that terminates at the sub-audible cull depth (strictly inside the doc-05 depth budget) and is strictly smaller than the closure the same scene warms in Flat mode; the warmed set equals the culled tree the mixer walks, so the primed-ring drain stays byte-identical between worker_count==0 and worker_count>0 with silence_mixed==0 (extends 12-audio#spatial-sub-audible-cull-terminates-recursion and 12-audio#lookahead-warms-recursive-contributor-closure)

The lookahead warming descent (LookaheadRing::descend) reconstructs, per edge, the same Spatialization the mixer's mix_layer builds -- composed listener = compose(parent_listener, layer.transform), viewport extent, accumulated attenuation product, sub-audible threshold -- and carries it onto the AudioRequest the pump submits for each warmed contributor, so a spatial-context-consuming contributor (a nested composition holding an off-center child) is warmed under the identical context the mixer pulls it with; therefore the threaded (worker_count>0) drain of a Spatial scene containing a nested-composition contributor is byte-identical to the inline (worker_count==0) spatial render and to a direct mix_composition oracle, with silence_mixed==0 and the mixer's pull for the warmed nested contributor a zero-dispatch cache hit; Flat (spatial absent) warms with no context -- a byte-exact no-op (extends 12-audio#lookahead-warms-recursive-contributor-closure and 12-audio#spatial-fill-cull-terminates-warming)

The block cache key carries a 64-bit spatial-context digest that is zero exactly when the request is Flat and otherwise reduces the full Spatialization (listener, viewport, accumulated attenuation, sub-audible threshold) under which the block is rendered; two distinct spatial contexts over the same (content, revision, block index, rate) therefore key to distinct cache slots, so a Spatial scene embedding one nested composition twice at different positions (same time map) drains -- for both worker_count==0 and worker_count>0 -- byte-identically to a fresh per-embedding mix_composition oracle, whereas the pre-digest key served one embedding block for both; the write-side warm digest equals the read-side pull digest for each edge so residency is preserved, and a Flat scene keys byte-identically to the pre-task key (extends 12-audio#spatial-warms-nested-with-pull-context and 12-audio#pull-audio-is-cache-first-single-settle)

Under the Spatial policy the interactive device monitor samples the live viewport camera on each mastering step and, on the step following a camera change, re-seeds the mix listener (and its uniform scale-attenuation clamp(max_scale(camera),0,1)) from it and flushes + reprimes the lookahead ring (the listener is part of the block key via the spatial-context digest, so blocks warmed under the old camera are stale); the drain output thereafter is byte-identical -- for both worker_count==0 and worker_count>0 -- to a static-seed run with LookaheadRingConfig::spatial.listener set to the new camera, and the reprime does not re-seat the drain cursor (drain_realigns does not advance -- a camera reprime is distinct from a seek reprime, the playhead being unmoved); an absent camera_source or Flat seed leaves the drain byte-unchanged (extends 12-audio#spatial-attenuates-by-composed-scale and 12-audio#block-key-disambiguates-spatial-context)

An unchanging live camera issues no listener re-seed and no reprime across any number of mastering steps -- proven by the DeviceMonitor::listener_reseeds() behavioral counter (== 1, the initial seed) and drain_realigns() staying at the no-camera baseline, never a wall clock -- so a static scene costs nothing while audio still tracks a live zoom; a camera change increments listener_reseeds() by exactly 1 at that step, and an absent camera_source keeps it 0 for the run's lifetime

The Registry resolves a reverse-DNS kind id to exactly one factory and its metadata (human name, version), enumerates registered ids in registration order, and rejects an empty or duplicate id as a value (RegistryError), never a throw; a kind id is a stable persistent identifier a serialization format references.
The canonical serialization of a given pinned document version is byte-identical across runs and across re-serialization -- sorted keys, fixed number formatting, defaults omitted
Serializing a held DocStatePtr emits that revision's object graph, unaffected by transactions committed after the pin

Loading the canonical writer output into a fresh document and re-serializing it reproduces the input byte-for-byte -- the reader is the exact inverse of the writer for the envelope, composition, and bottom-to-top core-owned layer placement, supplying each field's still/identity default where the writer omitted the key

A load installs the reconstructed graph as the document's version-0 baseline with an empty journal (via Model::load_baseline, not a transact), so immediately after load the revision is 0 and an undo is a no-op that never reverts the freshly-loaded document to empty

A document whose arbc.format is a major the reader does not know is rejected wholesale (ReaderError::UnknownFormatMajor) with no document mutation -- the target model stays at its empty revision-0 baseline; within a known major, unknown fields are preserved-and-ignored, not an error

LoadContext resolves relative references against the document base URI and deduplicates by resolved identity -- the same reference (or any reference resolving to the same URI) yields one shared ResolvedRef and one cache entry, a distinct reference yields a distinct one -- so doc-05 cross-file shared-content semantics survive persistence keyed on resolved-URI identity

A content body whose kind has no registered codec deserializes to a PlaceholderContent that preserves the original kind, kind_version, params, and inputs -- and any unknown fields -- verbatim, and re-serializes to the canonical form of that body byte-for-byte (sorted keys, canonical numbers); no field is dropped, reordered destructively, or coerced, so a missing plugin never destroys data (doc 08 Principles 2, 4, 5)

A kind with a codec registered in the serialize-owned codec table round-trips its params object through content_body_from_json -> live Content -> content_body_to_json byte-equivalent, and dispatch by reverse-DNS kind id selects that codec rather than the placeholder -- the codec seam real kinds plug into (doc 08 Principle 1 delta)

A PlaceholderContent with at least one bound input reports identity() == 0 so the compositor serves input 0 unchanged (a missing fade plugin degrades to an unfaded clip, not a hole); with no bound input it renders a bounded diagnostic fill and never faults (doc 08 Principle 6, doc 13)

An operator's core-owned input edges serialize as an inputs array beside params, order-significant (slot i is input index i) and omitted when empty; the content-aware writer walks Content::inputs() to emit it and the reader rebuilds the graph so the reconstructed root's inputs() yields the same children, nesting inline for chains (doc 08 Principle 6, doc 13)

Content referenced two or more times across the document's reachable graph is hoisted once into a document-level contents table under a deterministic first-encounter ordinal id and referenced by {"$ref": id} at every use site (an inputs slot or a layer's content position); singly-referenced content stays inline, and on load every $ref to one id resolves to a single shared live Content (intra-document dedup, not a fan-out into copies)

A $ref whose id is absent from the contents table, or one that closes an operator-input cycle, is a ReaderError::UnresolvableReference value surfaced with no model mutation (revision stays 0) and no nlohmann exception -- references are validated before the baseline install, so a bad graph never partially loads (doc 08 Principle 6, Decision 7)

A runtime Document carrying built-in-kind content (org.arbc.solid, org.arbc.tone) on placed layers serializes to canonical .arbc bytes byte-for-byte, and reloading those bytes into a fresh Document and re-saving reproduces them byte-identical -- the L5 load<->save integration that makes a built-in-kind document a file you can save and reopen (runtime.document_serialize, doc 08)

The runtime built-in codecs for org.arbc.solid and org.arbc.tone round-trip their params (solid: premultiplied Rgba; tone: frequency_hz + amplitude) through a Document save->load: the reconstructed content's fields equal the originals, dispatch by reverse-DNS kind id selects the codec (not a PlaceholderContent), and the sink stamps the KindBridge-interned kind into the pinned ContentRecord (runtime.document_serialize, doc 08 Principle 1)

The runtime built-in operator codecs for org.arbc.fade and org.arbc.crossfade round-trip an operator graph through the .arbc format with params and input edges intact: fade/crossfade save to a params-only body while the core routing re-derives the inputs array from Content::inputs(), a shared input content used two or more times dedups into the contents table under a single {"$ref": id} and reloads to one live Content reached at every site, and each operator reconstructs as the live built-in (not a PlaceholderContent) -- byte-exact and reload->resave identical (runtime.operator_codecs, doc 08 Principles 1/6)

The fade/crossfade DeserializeFn reconstructs the operator from its params plus the already-built input children, adopting them at construction in order-significant slots (fade slot 0; crossfade from=0, to=1); an input child is owned by Document with no ObjectId and mints no ContentRecord while the operator layer root binds an ObjectId/ContentRecord, and a wrong input arity is a ReaderError value leaving the model unmutated (runtime.operator_codecs Decisions 2/4)

load_document returns a ReaderError value or succeeds on arbitrary input bytes -- well-formed or hostile alike -- and never throws a JSON/C++ exception, aborts, or reads out of bounds; on any successful load the output re-serializes to a canonical fixed point (serialize(load(serialize(load(x)))) == serialize(load(x))), the differential-determinism invariant a libFuzzer harness over the untrusted .arbc loader pins (doc 08:143-149, reader.hpp:21-26)

A document with non-canonical or arbitrary contents ids and unsorted keys re-serializes to canonical byte-exact output: ids collapse to first-encounter ordinals over the canonical layers-then-inputs traversal and keys sort, so a hand-authored file is normalized on re-serialization -- canonicalization, not data loss (doc 08:105-131, sharing.md Decision 2)

Within a known format major, a sibling field this build does not name is preserved-and-ignored at EVERY tier of the document and re-emitted verbatim in canonical key order -- the envelope beside arbc.format, the document root beside composition/contents, the composition beside layers, inside the known working_space and time_map sub-objects, the layer (doc 08's own example 'name' field), a standalone contents-table body, and a known kind's params interior alike -- so serialize(load(x)) == x byte-exact for a canonical document carrying unknowns anywhere, and re-serialization is idempotent; a newer tool's or a plugin's sidecar annotation survives an older build's autosave (doc 08 Principle 4)

The writer builds its known object FIRST and merges the preserved stash into it, so a stashed key that collides with a key the core emits LOSES and the known value wins outright, recursing where both sides are objects -- working_space.primaries (read-ignored by the reader, emitted by the writer) round-trips as exactly one key carrying the writer's value, never the stashed one; 'unknown' is decided by key NAME alone, so a known key carrying a malformed value stays known (the reader's leniency substitutes its default and the bad value is not preserved), and a stash whose bytes fail to re-parse degrades to 'no preserved fields' rather than to a ReaderError or SerializeError (doc 08:96, Decisions 2/4)

An unknown key inside a REGISTERED kind's params round-trips byte-exact while the codec still produces the live Content (not a placeholder): the core recovers the remainder by differencing the codec's own re-serialization against the input params at LOAD time, so the residual is exactly the key set the codec did not consume and codec authors (built-in and plugin alike) write no preservation code; freezing the diff at load is what makes it safe -- clearing a param the codec DID consume (org.arbc.fade's optional params.in) re-saves with the key omitted rather than resurrecting the stale value, which a save-time diff against a stashed raw params would do (doc 08 Principle 4, Decision 4)

A document is a graph of compositions: the root keeps its home at the root `composition` key and holds the reserved ordinal "0", every OTHER reachable composition is emitted into a document-level `compositions` table under a non-semantic first-encounter ordinal ("1".."N") re-derived from graph structure on every save, and a nesting content names its child through the core-owned `composition` field beside kind/inputs/params -- which the core reads off `Content::composition_ref()` and re-derives itself, never through the kind's params (the kind's SerializeFn is not even consulted for it). A two-composition document round-trips byte-exact and re-serializes idempotently, sharing spans compositions (one content reached from two of them hoists into ONE contents entry referenced by $ref at both use sites and reloads to ONE live Content), and a table entry no reference reaches is ignored on load and dropped on save -- canonicalization, not data loss (doc 08 Principle 7).

A composition cycle is legal and serializes directly as data: a composition that embeds ITSELF names the root's reserved ordinal ("composition": "0") and emits no `compositions` key at all, and an A-embeds-B-embeds-A cycle emits B under "1" with B's nesting body carrying "composition": "0" -- both load, terminate, and re-serialize byte-exact. The reader terminates because it pre-allocates each composition's ObjectId BEFORE building that composition's layers, so a back-edge resolves immediately without re-entry. A composition cycle is NOT an operator-input cycle: a nesting content SHARED across the cycle (hoisted into contents, $ref at both use sites) loads successfully, while an actual $ref input cycle in the same document shape still returns ReaderError::UnresolvableReference -- the two cycle notions keep separate state and neither contaminates the other (doc 08 Principles 6/7, doc 05).

A missing plugin never orphans the composition it embeds (doc 08 Principle 2): an UNKNOWN-kind content body carrying a `composition` reference loads to a PlaceholderContent that carries the RESOLVED child ObjectId on composition_ref() -- the id is stripped from the placeholder's verbatim stored body exactly as `inputs` is, because both are core-owned graph structure the writer re-derives -- so the child composition stays reachable from the writer's walk, survives the save under a freshly-derived ordinal, and the whole document re-saves byte-exact through the full runtime load/save path with no codec for that kind in existence. Leaving the load-time ordinal in the opaque body would instead pin a stale id that renumbering can silently repoint at a different composition.

The composition-table read errors are values and the model is unmutated: a body whose `composition` id is absent from the `compositions` table (and is not the root's "0") is ReaderError::UnresolvableReference at /compositions/<id>, and a `compositions` entry keyed "0" -- claiming the root's reserved ordinal, which would otherwise silently delete a composition its author wrote -- is ReaderError::MalformedField at /compositions/0. Both are raised with no throw, before any DocState mutation: pre-allocating composition ObjectIds bumps only a monotonic counter and installs no record, so after a failed load the target Model still reports revision() == 0, contains(id) == false for every allocated id, and no CompositionRecord (doc 08 Principle 7; the load-installs-version-0-baseline discipline).

The built-in org.arbc.nested codec round-trips a REAL NestedContent -- not a test double -- byte-exact through a live Document and the L5 save_document/load_document facade: a plain nesting document (child composition emitted under compositions["1"], the nesting body naming it through the core-owned "composition" field), a Droste self-cycle, and a shared child named by two nesting contents all re-serialize to identical bytes and re-load to a live NestedContent whose child() is the composition the reader pre-allocated. The codec owns ONLY params, and NestedContent has none: serialize emits an empty params object and never the child id (the writer re-derives it from graph structure after the codec returns), deserialize is NestedContent(composition) and consumes no inputs, an absent child is ReaderError::MissingRequiredField at /composition, and a non-nested content handed to serialize is SerializeError::CodecFailed -- both values, never a throw. For an EXTERNAL child the split inverts: the reference is kind-owned, so serialize emits {"ref": <the authored URI>} and deserialize consumes it (runtime.nested_external_ref). Either way every params key the codec did NOT consume is preserved through the core's load-time residual diff (doc 08 Principles 1/4/7, docs 05/08).

A nesting content's input edges are a PROJECTION of its child composition, not authored data, and are never persisted: a content answering a non-null resolvable composition_ref() emits NO inputs array and the write traversal does not descend its inputs(), so the child's contents are reached through the compositions table alone and loading rebuilds the projection from the child. This makes a save a pure function of the DOCUMENT: NestedContent::inputs() is memo-derived from the child's layers and is non-empty exactly when bind_operators has attached it -- the state every rendered frame of an interactive session and every frame of an offline export leaves it in -- yet a save taken while a live OperatorBindingScope is held (even concurrently, on another thread, TSan clean and with no new lock) is BYTE-IDENTICAL to the unbound save, emitting no inputs, no contents table and no $ref. Descending them instead would count the child's layer contents twice, hoisting them into contents behind $refs that describe no authored sharing, and for a Droste scene would emit a $ref closing an operator-input cycle -- which Principle 6 forbids and the reader rejects, writing a file that will not load (doc 08 Principle 7, doc 13).

A kind names its child through composition_ref() or takes authored inputs, never both -- and a content body carrying BOTH a "composition" reference and an "inputs" array is ReaderError::MalformedField at the body's own path, however that body is reached (a layer's content position or a standalone contents-table entry), and whether the inputs array is populated or empty. Rejecting it beats silently dropping one of the two edge sets the author wrote, the preference doc 08 states twice in the principle this amends. The error is raised BEFORE either edge set is resolved, so no child is ever sunk and the model is left unmutated: revision() == 0, no CompositionRecord, and no exception escapes. The writer has never emitted such a body -- only a hand-authored or hostile file can carry one (doc 08 Principle 7's corollary, runtime.nested_codec Decision 2).

The production plugin loader's ARBC_PLUGIN_PATH scan is genuinely opt-in: with the variable unset or empty scan_plugin_path() performs zero dlopen attempts and zero filesystem access, leaving the registry size() unchanged and reporting loaded == 0; with it set to a plugin directory the scan loads each shared-library entry in deterministic lexicographic order and the out-of-lib kind (org.arbc.imageseq) becomes resolvable in the host's Registry.

Explicit host registration wins over the opt-in scan: a kind registered explicitly (via load_plugin or a direct Registry::add) before scan_plugin_path() is not clobbered when the scan re-encounters the same kind id -- the collision surfaces as a per-entry DuplicateId outcome in the scan report and the original factory is left intact (the scan is additive and DuplicateId-guarded, never last-writer-wins).

Every plugin-loader failure is a value across the boundary, never an exception or abort: load_plugin on a nonexistent path yields PluginLoadError::CannotOpen, on a shared library lacking arbc_plugin_register yields MissingEntryPoint, a kind whose id is already registered yields DuplicateId, and a direct Registry::add of a duplicate id yields RegistryError::DuplicateId -- each raised with no throw (REQUIRE_NOTHROW) and carrying a captured dlerror()-style diagnostic where applicable.

A bulk payload is allocated page-aligned from a power-of-two size class (round-up with a one-page floor); same-size payloads share one size-class store and distinct-class payloads get distinct stores, and the 1 MiB default raster tile hits an exact rung with zero internal waste

A big-block blob is shared across owners by reference count (every owner peeks identical bytes; retain/release write no data page) and is reclaimed to its class free pool when the last owner releases, the freed slot reused by the next same-size allocation with no capacity growth

org.arbc.raster's tile-pixel payloads are allocated from a per-content BigBlockPool (page-aligned, size-classed) with the tile table holding BlockSlotRefs in slabs; a level tile handle resolves through peek to exactly edge*edge*4*sizeof(float) page-aligned bytes, untouched tiles are shared across CoW versions by the pool per-slot refcount, and dropping a no-longer-shared version returns its blob slots to the class free pool (reused by the next same-size allocation with total_bytes_reserved flat)

Reconstructing a tiled payload is TILEWISE (doc 15): a raster load streams each persisted level-0 blob straight into its pool tile through RasterStore::build_from_tiles' per-tile fill callback, and never materializes the dense w*h*16 working buffer the pre-tilewise route handed to RasterContent's DecodedImage constructor (384 MB transient for a 24 MP layer -- a spare copy of the largest layer, on top of the tile table the load is legitimately building). Asserted as a BEHAVIORAL COUNTER over a counting global operator new, never a wall-clock and never an RSS sample (doc 16 tier 4): across a load of the same document geometry at 4x4 and at 16x16 tiles -- a 16x image-area increase -- both the LARGEST SINGLE heap allocation on the load path and the TRANSIENT HIGH-WATER (peak live bytes minus the pool's ChunkSource reservations and the tile table's index slabs, which are the RESIDENT and legitimately O(image) part) stay within one tile blob plus a FIXED slack, invariant in tile count. The fill sees exactly one tile at a time, exactly edge*edge*4 floats, in ascending flat row-major order (the order the codec's blobs array and its hash memo are positionally keyed on), and a fill that DECLINES abandons the build with no TileTable constructed and every blob it allocated returned to the pool

Each of the six in-lib kinds (org.arbc.solid/tone/raster/nested/fade/crossfade) also builds as a CI-only shared library whose extern "C" arbc_plugin_register registers exactly that one kind id into a Registry (size() grows by exactly 1, no transitively-dragged registration); dlopening it through the production PluginHost yields a factory that constructs a working Content whose facets (audio() pointer identity, editable() second-base-subobject adjustment), host-side service injection (attach/detach with a PullService and Backend implemented in the host image, so dispatch crosses the boundary in both directions), description methods, conformance-suite behavior, and rendered pixels are byte-identical to an identically-configured in-lib instance -- the in-lib kinds' only exercise of the runtime plugin path (doc 17:129-132, doc 03:233-234)

A nested content whose body carries a kind-owned params.ref URI loads and RENDERS the .arbc project that URI names -- M8's "external nested projects load", end to end over a real FilesystemAssetSource (the tree's first AssetSource) and real files. The reference resolves through LoadContext's base-URI joining, its bytes come back through the AssetSource hook, and its composition graph is installed into the HOST document's own Model as an ordinary child composition subtree whose root ObjectId becomes the NestedContent's child: "external" is PROVENANCE, not a different runtime representation, so render, audio, aggregate revision, damage routing and tile caching need no change whatsoever. The proof is an ORACLE, not a hand-typed constant: the external scene renders BYTE-IDENTICALLY to the same scene authored in-document with the child in the compositions table -- doc 05:47-52's "the same mechanism plus a loader; from the compositor's perspective there is no difference between an inline child and an external one", asserted at the pixel (docs 05, 08 Principle 3).

An external composition cycle loads as a FINITE graph rather than recursing forever or being rejected: cycles are data, not errors (doc 08:151-156 -- "composition cycles ride the compositions table OR an external params.ref URI -- neither is a contents/$ref edge"). Termination is ALLOCATE-BEFORE-PARSE, the in-document Droste knot-cut lifted across documents: the loader takes the child's root composition ObjectId from Model::allocate_id() and records resolved-URI -> id in its dedup map BEFORE parsing the child's bytes, so a back-edge resolves to the IN-FLIGHT composition instead of re-loading it. a.arbc -> b.arbc -> a.arbc loads with b fetched exactly ONCE and a never re-fetched, b's nested child resolving to a's own ROOT composition, and the cyclic graph's metadata walk and render both terminating under the sub-pixel cull. A document referencing ITSELF collapses onto the in-document Droste case exactly, because the top-level load seeds the map with its own base URI -> its own root id -- zero asset requests. A hostile ACYCLIC chain, which dedup cannot bound, is capped at k_external_ref_depth_cap links (doc 05, doc 08 Principle 7).

An external reference that cannot be loaded -- no AssetSource installed, a missing or unreadable file, bytes that are not a valid .arbc, or a load-time depth-cap overrun -- is reported as UNAVAILABLE, not as a read error: the embedding NestedContent loads with a NULL child, keeps its ref verbatim, describes and renders as the doc-05 placeholder, and the PARENT LOAD SUCCEEDS. A project must not become unopenable because a widget file moved. It is not demoted to an unknown-kind placeholder either -- the kind is perfectly well known and its reference must stay live, editable and re-resolvable, so a user who restores the file and reloads gets their scene back. The re-save is a fixed point: the document round-trips byte-identically with the ref intact, arbitrarily many open/save cycles with the file still missing. The asymmetry with a dangling in-document composition id (a read error) is deliberate: self-inconsistent bytes are a malformed document, whereas a missing external file is a condition of the environment that may resolve later (doc 05:50-52, doc 08 Principle 3).

A loaded external child is saved back as a REFERENCE, never inlined -- the data-loss hazard this rule exists to close. Once an external child actually renders, its composition_ref() resolves, and a writer that did not know better would walk the OTHER document's contents into this one's contents table and emit "composition": "1" in place of the URI: open, save, and the authored reference is gone, replaced by a frozen copy. So a content answering a non-empty Content::external_composition_ref() (doc 03) is named by that URI and by nothing else: the core emits NEITHER an inputs array NOR a composition field for it, and the write traversal (live and pinned-snapshot alike) descends NEITHER its inputs() nor its child composition -- no compositions key, no contents key, none of the child's contents. The AUTHORED string round-trips, never an absolutised path, so a project directory stays relocatable and the output stays byte-stable; two nested contents may therefore share one child composition while carrying different authored spellings. And the bytes do not depend on LOAD state any more than on binding state: a document saved with the widget file missing is byte-identical to the same document saved with it present (doc 08 Principle 3, Principle 7's third corollary).

A content body naming its child BOTH by a core-owned "composition" reference AND by a kind's external "params.ref" URI names one child two contradictory ways, and is ReaderError::MalformedField at /params/ref -- the third corollary of Principle 7's "a kind names its child through composition_ref() or takes authored inputs, never both". Rejecting it beats silently preferring one and dropping the other, the preference doc 08 states twice. The check lives in deserialize_nested, NOT in the core reader's RefResolver::build (which catches the sibling composition + inputs case), because params is kind territory and the core may not read its semantics -- and it surfaces through the codec's existing failure value, so no exception escapes and the writer, which has never emitted such a body, is unaffected. A present-but-non-string ref is treated leniently as absent instead: it is then a param the codec did not consume, so it rides the residual diff and round-trips verbatim, and no hostile input turns a mistyped key into a load failure (doc 08 Principle 7, runtime.nested_external_ref Decision 7).

decompress_blob(compress_blob(b), b.size()) returns b byte-for-byte for every input class the tile store hands the compressor: empty input; an all-zero tile (the untouched-region case, which must collapse to a tiny frame rather than cost a megabyte on disk); a flat constant-colour tile; a high-entropy incompressible photographic tile, whose compressed frame is legitimately LARGER than its input -- so the seam sizes its destination at ZSTD_compressBound, never at the input length, and a seam that did the latter would fail on exactly the tiles that are 93% of a painting's bytes; and a tile at the real geometry (edge*edge*k_tile_channels*sizeof(float) = 1 MiB at the default 256 edge in the rgba32f working format, 512 KiB at the rgba16f storage default). Because the store's content hash is over the UNCOMPRESSED bytes, this round-trip -- not the frame's bytes -- is the whole of what the format depends on from the compressor, so its version and level stay free (doc 08 Principle 8, serialize.zstd_dep Decision 1)

A corrupt, truncated, or hostile tile blob yields an arbc::expected ERROR VALUE from decompress_blob: no exception escapes, no abort, and no allocation beyond the caller's declared bound. decompress_blob takes its output size from the caller -- the tile geometry the document declares and independently validates -- and NEVER from the frame header, which on the loader's untrusted, libFuzzer-driven path is attacker-controlled data. Covered: random bytes that are not a zstd frame; an empty blob; a valid frame truncated mid-stream; a well-formed frame whose content is SHORTER than the caller's bound (a clean decode that fails the length check -- SizeMismatch); one whose content is LONGER (zstd refuses rather than writing past the destination); and -- the one that matters -- a frame whose header advertises a 64 GiB content size against a one-tile bound, which fails as a value rather than attempting the allocation, closing what would otherwise be a one-line remote denial of service in the loader (doc 08 Dependency note, serialize.zstd_dep Constraint 7 / Decision 6)

A deferring AssetSource, whose on_ready fires after load_document has already returned, leaves the embedding NestedContent holding a VALID child ObjectId that names no CompositionRecord: the parent load succeeds at revision 0 without blocking, and the content describes and renders as the doc-05 placeholder -- pending, not unavailable, and not an error. When the bytes arrive, the writer-thread settle installs the child's composition graph under that SAME pre-allocated id through one ordinary transaction, publishing a NEW revision (never a republished baseline -- the host root id and the host's own layers survive) and flushing, in that same commit, damage naming the EMBEDDING content, whole-object and all-time -- which doc 02's Refine step turns into a follow-up frame. The scene then renders BYTE-IDENTICALLY to the same scene loaded through an inline source, and the nested metadata memo re-keys across the transition (the empty placeholder while pending, the child's real bounds after), answering from the memo on repeated queries either side of it. Empty bytes from a source that DID answer remain unavailable (a null child), so the pending/unavailable split is a property of whether the source answered, never of the bytes being empty; a source that never answers leaves the child pending forever and the document still saves, renders and closes cleanly (doc 05, doc 02:69-71).

Dedup and the depth cap survive the async boundary, because the child's ObjectId is recorded in the resolved-identity map before the FETCH is issued, not merely before the parse: a deferring a -> b -> a cycle terminates with b fetched exactly once and a never re-fetched, resolving b's child to a's root; a deferring a -> b -> c chain lands across successive settles (a grandchild's request cannot be issued until its parent's bytes are parsed) with each settle draining its own ready queue to quiescence; and a hostile acyclic chain of deferring links is still capped at k_external_ref_depth_cap, because each pending entry restores the depth at which its reference was reached rather than resuming from zero -- a live recursion counter is meaningless across an async boundary, and resetting it would silently uncap the chain in exactly the configuration (a network source) where the cap matters most. A callback firing after its Document is destroyed drops its bytes and faults nothing: the completion queue is held by shared_ptr and every on_ready closure captures a weak_ptr (doc 05, doc 08 Principle 7).

A commit writes the published revision as the per-object revision stamp of exactly the objects it touched, riding the copy-on-write each mutation already performs; every untouched object keeps its prior stamp with no write at all, by the same structural sharing that keeps its record (#commit-shares-untouched-structure), so the stamp costs no traversal and O(path depth) still bounds a commit

An undo publishes a new document revision (revision +1) but RESTORES each touched object's prior revision stamp rather than advancing it, because navigate() republishes the stored owning edge by SlotRef identity and never re-creates a record; the restored record is byte-identical to the one the pre-edit tiles were rendered from, so those tiles become legitimately reachable again, and commit -> undo -> redo returns every stamp to its post-commit value

Over a 60-commit brush-stroke loop (one transaction per dab) on one layer of a multi-layer scene, every unedited layer's tile keys are unchanged across all 60 commits, so a pan after the stroke composites them from the cache with zero dispatched renders (requests_issued delta 0 for the untouched layers), where a document-global revision key left every tile outside the dab cached under a dead key and re-rendered the whole viewport cold

Reopening a workspace file opens the document at max(persisted per-object stamp) + 1, folding the max into the reachability walk that already rebuilds refcounts over every record, so a commit in the recovered session can never mint a stamp already carried by a record still reachable from a journal before-edge or a pinned snapshot -- which would key two different renderings of one object alike

A content's revision contribution folds its own record stamp with the arrangement stamp of the composition it names (that composition's record stamp plus every member layer's record stamp, inline arm and spill chain alike), so a reorder, an attach/detach, or a member layer's transform/opacity/span/time-map edit -- none of which touches any child CONTENT, and none of which the inputs() fold can see -- still changes the embedder's composed-result key and re-renders it; a contribution built from the content stamp alone serves the pre-edit composite

The aggregate fold sums a bijective 64-bit mix of each reachable node's contribution rather than the raw values, so two inputs whose stamps move in opposite directions by equal amounts (an undo/redo interleaving; a membership edit swapping a high-stamp layer for a low-stamp one) cannot cancel to a previously-seen aggregate and serve another configuration's composed tile; the fold stays order-independent and visits each reachable node exactly once. This makes the "iff" of #aggregate-revision-folds-reachable-inputs true, which the raw sum delivered only while every contribution carried the same document-global value

The stale-revision fallback probes the prior stamp of THAT content -- the stamp the last frame composited it under, aggregate for an operator layer and leaf stamp otherwise -- not a document-global prior revision, which under per-object stamps names no content's prior stamp and would miss unconditionally; a content the frame has never composited has no prior stamp and no stale tier, preserving #degraded-fallback-preference-order

The installed arbc package exposes the conformance suite as the optional `testing` component: a foreign CMake project that find_package(arbc CONFIG REQUIRED COMPONENTS testing) against a staged install prefix links arbc::arbc + arbc::testing, calls arbc::contract_tests(factory) on a Content it defines itself, and passes -- with no reference to the arbitrarycomposer source or build tree

libarbc never links arbc-testing (doc 17:14): find_package(arbc CONFIG) without COMPONENTS testing succeeds and yields a usable arbc::arbc on a machine where Catch2 cannot be found (CMAKE_DISABLE_FIND_PACKAGE_Catch2=ON, so the negative is real and not incidental to the CI image), and arbc::testing is not defined in that configuration

The version the library reports at link time (arbc::linked_version()), the version its public header declares at compile time (ARBC_VERSION_MAJOR/MINOR/PATCH, arbc::compiled_version()), and the version its CMake package advertises (arbc_VERSION, from PROJECT_VERSION via arbcConfigVersion.cmake) are one and the same triple -- derived mechanically from the single project(... VERSION ...) declaration, with no second literal anywhere in the tree -- in the build tree and, independently, through a staged install prefix consumed by a foreign project, which is also what proves arbc/version.hpp is INSTALLED at the include root and not merely generated

An org.arbc.image content returns nullptr from editable(): it exposes NO Editable facet, and that omission is the load-bearing property, not a gap to be filled later (doc 03:256-268) -- because you cannot paint on a photograph, retouching one must stack an editable org.arbc.raster above a referenced org.arbc.image, which is what makes non-destructive editing structural rather than conventional; the read-only shape it entails holds too (Static, time_extent()/quantize_time() nullopt, no audio facet, no operator inputs, no composition ref, and render_thread_safe() true because an immutable pyramid makes render a pure read)

N org.arbc.image layers whose AUTHORED references differ in spelling but resolve to one URI (bg.png, ./bg.png, assets/./bg.png) issue EXACTLY ONE decode and share one immutable pyramid -- the plugin-side pyramid cache is keyed on the RESOLVED identity, not the spelling (doc 08:116-122), and that dedup is unconditional. Re-loading or re-rendering an unchanged image issues ZERO further decodes WHILE ITS PYRAMID REMAINS RESIDENT; under a byte budget (15-memory-model#image-pyramid-evicts-under-byte-budget) an EVICTED image costs EXACTLY ONE further decode on the next pull, and not one more -- residency, not identity, is what the budget changed. Asserted as a behavioral counter (decodes_issued()) and never as a wall-clock timing (doc 16:54-62); the property survives concurrent construction of several contents against one URI, because the decode stays under the cache mutex

An org.arbc.image layer round-trips byte-exact through save->load->save carrying ONLY its authored URI: the emitted params is exactly {"source": <uri>} and nothing else -- no pixel data, no tile table, no intrinsic size enters the document (doc 08 Principle 3, "an imported image has a file it came from, so it serializes as a URI and nothing more") -- and it is the AUTHORED reference that re-saves, never absolutised and never rewritten to the resolved URI, so a project directory stays relocatable and the bytes stay stable (doc 08:124-137)

A missing, unreadable, or undecodable external image asset -- and a LoadContext with NO AssetSource installed at all -- all load SUCCESSFULLY rather than failing the read (doc 08:126-134, doc 05:77-83): the content keeps its authored ref verbatim, reports empty bounds and renders nothing (there being no intrinsic extent to draw a placeholder over), and the layer re-saves byte-identically, so a missing external file is a condition of the environment that may resolve later rather than data loss

An org.arbc.image render answers with a non-transient content-provided surface whose extent is the REQUESTED REGION AT THE ACHIEVED SCALE -- never the whole decoded frame (doc 09:157-160) -- so a cache insert copies one tile rather than one image; asserted over a fixture materially larger than the compositor's tile size, at several scale rungs and at non-origin regions, with the surface carrying the composition working-space tag and its release callback returning it to the kind's free list

The stb-class decode dependency org.arbc.image parses foreign image bytes with resolves only in the plugin tier (the shared vendored archive, arbc-plugin-image's impl archive and its loadable module) and no decode symbol appears in libarbc or arbc-testing, so a decoder never enters an embedder's link line -- the codec line is a DECODER line: the kind's serialize codec lives in runtime and crosses nothing, because it parses only our own JSON and a URI string (doc 17 "The codec line", doc 10:29)

An AssetSource that does not answer INSIDE request() leaves an org.arbc.image content PENDING, not unavailable: the parent document loads successfully at revision 0, the layer is present with its authored ref preserved verbatim and EMPTY bounds, and it renders nothing without faulting. When the source later answers with decodable bytes, a writer-thread settle publishes EXACTLY ONE new revision whose commit flushes EXACTLY ONE damage batch naming the image content (whole-object, all-time), the content's bounds become the decoded extent, and the next frame composites pixels BYTE-IDENTICAL to the same document loaded through an inline-firing source -- doc 02's Refine step, driven by an ordinary revision bump and a damage route. A source answering TWICE for one URI installs once and publishes one revision (the second arrival finds nothing pending), and a pending image inside a pending nested child settles across two rounds of the one shared settle loop

Pending and unavailable are separated by WHETHER THE SOURCE ANSWERED, never by the bytes being empty: a source that answers inline with empty bytes yields an UNAVAILABLE content (no pending entry, no later revision, no damage), a LoadContext with no AssetSource installed at all yields the same (load_context.cpp fires the continuation inline), a DEFERRED arrival carrying empty or undecodable bytes settles to unavailable FOR FREE (no transaction, no revision, no damage -- install_asset returns false as a value, never a throw), and a source that never answers leaves the content PENDING forever, where the document still renders, still saves byte-identically and still closes cleanly. An arrival firing after the Document is destroyed installs nothing and faults nothing: the on_ready closure holds a weak_ptr and drops its bytes

What an org.arbc.image content publishes ATOMICALLY and AT MOST ONCE is its decoded EXTENT -- the geometry the compositor culls on -- monotonically (zero -> known, never reverting and never replaced), so a late install cannot make a layer flicker into and out of the composition. Its PIXELS are budgeted DERIVED data: the pyramid cache owns them, a byte budget evicts them, and a pull re-derives them byte-identically from the retained encoded source, so their history is legally non-null -> null -> non-null. render_thread_safe() stays true across BOTH -- across a concurrent late install AND across continuous eviction on every render thread -- on three legs: a Pyramid object is immutable for its entire life and eviction only drops a reference to one; a render holds an OWNING residency pin, so a concurrent eviction can never free pixels it is reading; and the cache is mutex-guarded. A worker therefore observes exactly two self-consistent states -- the empty one (empty bounds, culled) or a FULLY decoded pyramid it owns for the duration of the call -- and never a third: checked by comparing every non-empty render byte-for-byte against the reference, so a torn pin or a half-built pyramid surfaces as a pixel mismatch and not merely as a race report. Redundant installs never move a published extent and issue no second decode. Asserted under TSan, never as a wall-clock assertion

The plugin-side pyramid cache OWNS its decoded pyramids strongly (a byte budget over values the cache does not own frees nothing and is decoration) and bounds them by a CONFIGURABLE BYTE BUDGET, evicting LRU and skipping PINNED entries -- doc 02:268-284s tile-cache policy applied to a second population, and the one doc 15 names as a kinds decoded source asset. Under a budget admitting exactly two of three pyramids, a third decode evicts the least-recently-PINNED one (evictions() == 1, resident_bytes() <= budget), and recency is by PIN rather than by insert order, so an LRU over what renders actually READ is what chooses the victim. Re-pulling the evicted image issues EXACTLY ONE further decode and re-pulling a still-resident one issues ZERO. The budget is a SOFT TARGET: a pinned entry is never chosen as a victim while its pin is live, an entry evicted while pinned keeps its pixels readable and byte-correct through the owning pin, and a pyramid LARGER THAN THE ENTIRE BUDGET stays resident and renders -- correctness outranks the budget (doc 02:278-284), exactly as Journal never trims below one entry. bounds() of an EVICTED image is unchanged, non-empty, and costs zero decodes (it reads the retained extent, takes no pin and never decodes), because bounds() is on the compositors CULL path and an image that reported empty bounds when memory got tight would cull itself out of the composition and vanish; only a genuinely UNAVAILABLE asset has empty bounds, and the two states are distinguishable. Every assertion is a behavioral counter -- decodes_issued(), evictions(), resident_bytes() -- and never a wall-clock one (doc 16:54-62)

An evicted-then-re-decoded org.arbc.image renders BYTE-IDENTICAL pixels to the same image rendered while resident -- at native scale, on a decimated mip rung, on an Exact magnify and on a BestEffort clamp, with the scale-honesty flags unchanged too -- because Pyramid::decode is a pure function of the retained encoded bytes over medias frozen kernels. No new golden data is authored for it: the EXISTING golden suite and the ENTIRE contract conformance suite are re-run against a cache whose budget is one byte, so every pull evicts and every render re-decodes, and they must produce what they produced resident. And eviction is MODEL-INVISIBLE: it bumps NO revision, emits NO damage, and mutates no model state, so composed tiles keyed on (content id, revision, scale rung, tile coords) (doc 02:257) stay valid across it -- nothing a composed tile depends on changed. Bumping a revision here would orphan every cached tile in the document to announce that some pixels moved from RAM to recomputable. RESIDENCY IS NOT COMPOSITION STATE

org.arbc.raster persists THE TILE TABLE, NOT THE IMAGE (doc 08 Principle 8): the save walks the level-0 BlockSlotRef grid and encodes each tile independently, never materializing a dense buffer of the layer. Content-addressing is therefore real and not incidental, and the counters prove it where a round-trip cannot -- a dense-flatten implementation would round-trip perfectly and fail these. An untouched all-identical layer (a 4x4 grid of sixteen identical tiles) writes EXACTLY ONE blob and still names all sixteen in its `blobs` array, because the dedup lives in the NAME and not in the tile table -- the first save legitimately HASHES all sixteen, since RasterStore::build mints a fresh pool blob per tile and a slot-keyed memo cannot know two slots hold the same bytes without hashing them; the dedup is downstream of the hash, which is precisely why the blob count is 1 while the hash count is 16, and why a RE-save of the same layer hashes ZERO and writes ZERO. Two SEPARATE raster contents whose pixels are identical -- distinct pool slots, distinct tile tables -- write that blob ONCE, which is doc 08's cross-layer dedup (4.3x, the largest size lever in its table, above compression's 2.9x). Asserted on AssetSink::blobs_written() and RasterTileStore::tiles_hashed(), never on directory size and never on wall-clock (doc 16 tier 4). Blobs land under the 2-hex fan-out assets/tiles/<hh>/<hash>, which the store derives, so the JSON never names a blob path

Incremental save falls out of content-addressing as a CONSEQUENCE, and is asserted as a behavioral counter rather than a wall-clock time (doc 16 tier 4: wall-clock tests lie in CI, counters don't). Save a painted document of sixteen distinct tiles; apply one dab touching tile set T; re-save into the same asset directory: EXACTLY |T| blobs are written AND EXACTLY |T| tiles are hashed. The hash counter is the stronger of the two -- write-if-absent alone would give the right blob count while still re-hashing the whole document on every save, which is the difference between a gesture-cadence autosave and a lie. It holds because RasterStore::paint leaves every untouched tile's BlockSlotRef bit-identical (kinds.raster_pool_backing's structural sharing) and the memo is keyed on that ref and PINNED BY A REFCOUNT -- the generation tag is #ifndef NDEBUG-gated, so in release a recycled slot is bit-identical to a stale one and only an owning BlockRef can prove the bytes behind the key were never recycled. A save NEVER DELETES: the pre-dab blob stays on disk, because another version, another .arbc, or a concurrent editor may reference it

With storage_format rgba32f, load(save(D)) yields a TileTable whose every level-0 tile is BIT-IDENTICAL to D's, and re-serializing yields BYTE-IDENTICAL JSON. The params carry exactly five keys -- tiles (the authored base URI, resolved through the same LoadContext seam nested projects use, which is what keeps a project directory relocatable), edge, width, height, and a flat row-major `blobs` array of LEVEL-0 hashes -- and nothing else. The storage format rides the document's `arbc` meta block and NOT the layer's params, because the hash is over storage-format bytes and two layers at different formats would hash the same pixels to different names, quietly killing the cross-layer dedup the whole principle rests on. The re-save writes ZERO blobs and hashes ZERO tiles: every blob is already on disk under its name, and the load seeded the memo with the names it already knew. The compressed FRAME bytes are deliberately NOT goldened -- doc 08:353-361 reserves the right of a different zstd version or level to emit different bytes for the same tile, so a frame-byte golden would encode a promise the format does not make. The byte-shuffle IS ours and version-stable, so it gets its own byte-exact golden as a pure function

At the default storage_format rgba16f the FIRST save quantizes f32->f16 -- lossy, by the user's authored choice, and the honest reason 'byte-exact round-trip' is unqualified only at rgba32f. Thereafter it is a FIXED POINT: f16_to_float followed by f16_from_float is EXACT, so save -> load -> save re-derives the same storage bytes, hence an IDENTICAL hash set, hence ZERO new blobs written and byte-identical JSON. The default is omit-when-default in the envelope, so a document that never authored a storage format serializes byte-identically to a pre-tile-store one

Mip levels are DERIVED and are neither written nor read (doc 08 Principle 8): the params carry level 0 only -- a flat `blobs` array, not the array-of-arrays doc 08's example once showed, which would have implied persisted mips. NO blob is written for any level above 0, asserted by COUNT (the blobs on disk number exactly the level-0 tiles) and by the emitted NAME SET (the files on disk are exactly the names in `blobs`) -- asserted, not assumed. A load rebuilds the pyramid through RasterContent::from_tiles -- the TILEWISE construction path (kinds.raster_tilewise_load), which shares the ONE decimation chain with the dense DecodedImage constructor rather than copying it -- so every mip rung of a loaded document is BYTE-IDENTICAL to the pre-save document's, and to a dense build of the same pixels. That is sound for free because 14-data-model-and-editing#paint-mip-recompute-matches-full-rebuild already holds; this claim pins that the save/load path actually routes through it

A tile blob is SELF-VERIFYING, because its name is a hash of its own contents: decoding decompresses the frame, unshuffles it, hashes the resulting UNCOMPRESSED STORAGE bytes, and compares against the name the blob was fetched under. A truncated file, a bit-flipped frame, or a SUBSTITUTED blob (a perfectly valid frame that is simply not this tile's) is a ReaderError -- never a crash, never silent wrong pixels. The hash is over the uncompressed storage bytes and NOT over the compressed frame (doc 08:353-361, 'compression is a storage encoding, never content identity'), which is what lets a zstd version or level change emit different bytes for the same tile without renaming a single blob, invalidating the asset directory, or breaking dedup between two collaborators on different machines

Untrusted tile geometry is validated BEFORE it is used as an allocation bound (doc 08:440-442, extended from the frame header to the tile table). zstd_dep bounded decompression by the CALLER's declared size; that size is now derived from an edge/width/height read out of a possibly-hostile document, so the geometry itself has entered the threat model. Rejected as VALUES, before any allocation is sized by them: a non-positive, non-power-of-two, or absurd `edge` (including one beyond int32, which a narrowing get<int>() would silently truncate into something plausible -- hence the validation runs on int64); a width/height that is non-positive, absurd, or whose implied tiles_x * tiles_y exceeds the cap; a `blobs` array whose length is not EXACTLY tiles_x * tiles_y (padding it would invent pixels, truncating it would drop the user's); and a malformed hash string -- the one place a hostile document gets to name a FILE, so a name that is not 32 lowercase hex chars never reaches a URI resolver. The serialize.format_tests libFuzzer harness over the loader carries raster-layer seeds in its checked-in corpus

An explicit mark-and-sweep GC over a store with on-disk hash set D and caller-referenced set R deletes exactly D \ R and retains every blob in R -- asserted on the GcReport counters (deleted == |D \ R|, every hash in R still present after the run), never on directory size or wall-clock, and backed by a round-trip: loading the swept document is byte-identical to before GC because every referenced blob still decodes and verifies against its name (serialize.asset_gc, doc 08 § The asset directory)

Reclamation is a distinct, explicit operation and the save path never deletes: after saving a painted document, applying a later painted state, and re-saving, the pre-edit blobs of the overwritten tiles are still on disk (the never-delete rule); only then does an explicit GC with the current document as sole root delete exactly the now-orphaned pre-edit blobs while retaining the current tiles (serialize.asset_gc Decision 1, doc 08 L50-53)

When two .arbc files share one asset directory and GC is given both as roots (the convenience directory scan), a blob referenced only by the second document is NOT deleted and a blob referenced by neither IS -- the referenced set is the union across every preserved document, the cross-.arbc safety property that is the whole reason the sweep is explicit and caller-rooted rather than implicit on a single save (serialize.asset_gc Constraint 5, doc 08 L50-52)

GC enumerates and deletes only blobs under the resolved assets/tiles/ subtree whose basename passes is_tile_hash: an imported assets/bg.png and any non-hash-named file under tiles/ survive a sweep that reclaims an orphaned tile beside them -- imported images are referenced by URI, not content hash, and are outside this task's reference model (serialize.asset_gc Constraint 4/Decision 4)

A mark that cannot be fully computed deletes nothing: a root document that is unparseable, or whose params.blobs carries a non-hash entry, makes GC return a GcError value with ZERO deletions -- the on-disk set is bit-for-bit unchanged. Over-preservation on any doubt; a partial mark that then deleted would be data loss, so errors are values and the plan is validated in full before a single unlink (serialize.asset_gc Constraint 3/Decision 3)

GC reaches a fixed point in one pass: a second sweep immediately after a first, same roots, deletes zero blobs (report.deleted == 0). Re-running is a no-op, which is what makes a dry_run followed by a real run predictable -- the dry_run reports the identical deleted/bytes_reclaimed the real run reclaims while leaving the store untouched (serialize.asset_gc)

A visual frame renders exactly one composition's layers -- the members of the viewport's anchor composition, walked via for_each_layer_in -- and never the document-global leaf set; a nested child composition's layers are reached only by recursing through the enclosing NestedContent layer, so they are composited exactly once through that layer's embedding rather than also being double-drawn at top level with child-local transforms

The runtime WorkerPool runs a SECOND lane besides the leaf-only render lane: a generic, cache-free work job (submit_work over a WorkTask -- a plain movable-callable settling a caller-owned WorkCompletion), the substrate for the parallel tile-encode save (serialize.tile_store_parallel_save Decision 1). It shares the pool's threads, its poke/wait_completions park machinery, its drain_owner teardown, and its counters, but has NO per-content serialization gate because a work job is pure by construction; the leaf-only rule governs the RENDER lane, and the work lane is cache-free because its jobs touch only their own caller-owned buffers and the immutable pinned document, never the TileCache, so doc 02's "workers never touch the cache" holds for BOTH lanes. Driven directly: at worker_count == 0 a work job runs inline on the submitting thread and settles; at worker_count > 0 it runs OFF the calling thread and wakes a parked waiter; work jobs and renders submitted together close the ONE accounting identity tasks_submitted == tasks_completed + tasks_dropped + outstanding at quiescence; drain_owner waits out a started work job and purges an owner's queued ones (counted by tasks_dropped, left unsettled), touching no sibling owner's work; and queued work abandoned on stop is COUNTED, not silently lost. TSan-clean under the per-push gcc-tsan lane

The parallel tile-encode save is a pure THROUGHPUT change over the serial one: saving one painted document through a worker-backed TileEncodeDispatch (N > 1 pool workers fanning the per-tile hash+shuffle+compress) and through the inline dispatch produces BYTE-IDENTICAL output -- identical canonical .arbc JSON, an identical row-major params.blobs array, and an identical on-disk blob set (same hash-named files, same frame bytes) -- because the hash is over each tile's own uncompressed storage bytes and the blobs array is fixed row-major, so the output follows from the format and not from the completion order (serialize.tile_store_parallel_save Constraint 1, Decision 3; the serial golden is the oracle, the parallel run diffed against it). Every mutation -- memo, in-save/on-disk dedup, positional blobs[i], AssetSink write -- stays on the save thread; workers run only the pure encode. The incremental counters are executor-independent too: a re-save after one dab touching tile set T writes exactly |T| blobs (AssetSink::blobs_written()) and hashes exactly the rehashed tiles (RasterTileStore::tiles_hashed()) IDENTICALLY under worker_count in {0, N}, and an all-empty (ref-shared) layer dispatches at most one encode and writes one blob under a worker-backed save (the save-thread ref-level dedup). TSan-clean while a writer thread paints and drops versions concurrently, and executor-independent across many seeded worker-yield schedules

The parallel tile-encode fan-out is BOUNDED IN FLIGHT: a windowed submit/reap, not fire-all-then-join. Across a save of an image with many more distinct tiles than workers, the peak number of simultaneously-outstanding encode jobs (TileEncodeDispatch::peak_in_flight()) never exceeds the window bound O(worker_count) (TileEncodeDispatch::window(), a small multiple of the pool's worker count, exactly 1 for the inline executor), so a save's transient scratch is O(worker_count * tile) independent of image size and an autosave cannot flood the shared render pool with an image's worth of queued jobs (serialize.tile_store_parallel_save Constraint 4, the O(worker_count * tile) memory bound of doc 15). Asserted as a behavioral counter regardless of tile count -- never against wall-clock and never against directory size

The parallel tile-decode load is a pure THROUGHPUT change over the serial one: loading one saved document through a worker-backed TileDecodeDispatch (N > 1 pool workers fanning the per-tile decompress+unshuffle+verify-hash) and through the inline dispatch produces a TileTable whose every level-0 tile is BIT-IDENTICAL, and re-serializing each yields BYTE-IDENTICAL canonical .arbc JSON and an identical row-major params.blobs array (serialize.tile_store_parallel_load Constraint 1, Decision 3; the inline load is the oracle, the worker-backed run diffed against it). It follows from the format and not from the completion order: the hash verify is over each tile's own uncompressed storage bytes, the blobs array is fixed row-major, and the reap is strictly by ascending index, so completion order provably cannot reach the tile table. The FETCH (LoadContext::resolve + AssetSource read) and every pool write (BigBlockPool::allocate, the std::copy_n into the tile blob, the memo seed) stay on the loading thread; workers run only the pure decode over their own job-owned frame, so the load adds ZERO new TSan surface to the asset source. The memo state is executor-independent too: load(save(D)) yields a bit-identical tile table and a zero-blob, zero-hash re-save (RasterTileStore::tiles_hashed()/AssetSink::blobs_written() both zero) whether the load ran inline or across N workers. TSan-clean while N workers decode concurrently, and executor-independent across many seeded worker-yield schedules

The parallel tile-decode fan-out is BOUNDED IN FLIGHT: a windowed fetch/submit/reap look-ahead, not fetch-all-then-decode-all. Across a load of an image with many more tiles than workers, the peak number of simultaneously-outstanding decode jobs (TileDecodeDispatch::peak_in_flight()) never exceeds the window bound O(worker_count) (TileDecodeDispatch::window(), a small multiple of the pool's worker count, exactly 1 for the inline executor), so a load's transient scratch is O(worker_count * tile) independent of image size and opening a document cannot flood the shared render pool with an image's worth of queued decode jobs (serialize.tile_store_parallel_load Constraint 4, the O(worker_count * tile) memory bound of doc 15). Asserted as a behavioral counter regardless of tile count -- never against wall-clock and never against RSS
